title: Treasury Circular 230: Practice Before IRS
description: Master the rules governing practice before the Internal Revenue Service

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Circular 230 is the LAW for tax practitioners! It governs who can practice before the IRS and how they must behave. Violations can result in disbarment, suspension, or monetary penalties. This is heavily tested on REG!

sections.title: Who May Practice Before the IRS?
sections.content:
**"Practice" means:**
• Representing taxpayers
• Preparing and filing documents
• Communicating with IRS on taxpayer's behalf

**Authorized practitioners:**
• Attorneys (licensed, in good standing)
• CPAs (licensed, in good standing)
• Enrolled Agents (EAs)
• Enrolled Actuaries (limited to pension matters)
• Enrolled Retirement Plan Agents (limited)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Who Can Practice
sections.content:
**"ACE"** can practice:

**A**ttorneys
**C**PAs
**E**nrolled Agents

**These three have UNLIMITED practice rights!**

sections.title: Practitioner Duties & Restrictions
rows: Must exercise due diligence in preparing returns and documents
Must promptly submit information requested by IRS
Must advise client of errors and consequences
Cannot represent conflicting interests without disclosure and consent

sections.title: Standards for Tax Positions
sections.content:
**Advising positions on returns:**

**Standard:** Realistic possibility of being sustained on merits

**"Realistic possibility" = 1 in 3 (33%+) chance of success**

**Cannot advise positions that:**
• Lack reasonable basis
• Are frivolous
• Are solely for delay

sections.title: Written Advice Standards
sections.content:
**Must not give written advice that:**

• Is based on unreasonable assumptions
• Unreasonably relies on client representations
• Doesn't consider all relevant facts
• Reaches unreasonable conclusions

**Covered opinions** have additional requirements for tax shelter transactions

sections.title: Prohibited Conduct
content: Making false or misleading statements to IRS
Using threat or false accusations to coerce
Charging unconscionable fees
Unauthorized disclosure of client information
Negotiating refund checks (preparers only)
Soliciting in prohibited ways
Practicing while suspended or disbarred

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Refund Check Negotiation
sections.content:
**Tax return preparers CANNOT:**

• Endorse or negotiate client refund checks
• This applies even with client permission!

**Why?** Prevents preparer fraud

**Attorneys and CPAs representing clients in other matters CAN negotiate checks**—but not when acting as preparers!

sections.title: Sanctions for Violations
sections.content:
**OPR (Office of Professional Responsibility) can:**

• **Censure** - Public reprimand
• **Suspend** - Temporary loss of practice rights
• **Disbar** - Permanent loss of practice rights
• **Monetary penalty** - Up to $1,000 per violation

**Willful violations = Disbarment likely**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Circular 230 governs practice before the IRS
CPAs, attorneys, and EAs have unlimited practice rights
Must exercise due diligence in all tax matters
Tax positions need realistic possibility (33%+) of success
Cannot negotiate client refund checks
Violations can result in suspension or disbarment
Must advise clients of discovered errors

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Practice Before the IRS - Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following activities constitutes "practice" before the IRS, as defined by Treasury Circular 230?
options: Preparing a taxpayer's personal income tax return for a family member as a hobby without compensation.
Representing a taxpayer during an audit examination conducted by the IRS.
Providing general tax advice to a client regarding potential investment strategies.
Attending a tax seminar to enhance one's own knowledge of current tax laws.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Representing a taxpayer during an audit examination is a direct interaction with the IRS on the taxpayer's behalf, fitting the definition of 'practice' according to Circular 230. The other options do not involve direct representation or communication with the IRS.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which activity involves direct interaction with the IRS on behalf of a taxpayer.

reveal.question: What is the minimum percentage chance of success that constitutes a "realistic possibility" of being sustained on its merits for a tax position, according to Circular 230?
reveal.answer: A "realistic possibility" requires at least a 33% (1 in 3) chance of success on the merits.

sections.title: Prohibited Advice: Reasonable Basis vs. Frivolous

rows.label: Definition
values: The position is arguable but is unlikely to prevail in court. It is a higher standard than not being negligent, but lower than realistic possibility.
The position is patently improper and is not supported by any reasonable legal argument. It is often intended to impede or obstruct the tax system.

rows.label: Acceptability
values: Advising such a position is prohibited unless the taxpayer is adequately informed of the potential penalties and the position is disclosed to the IRS.
Advising such a position is strictly prohibited, regardless of disclosure or client awareness.

rows.label: Penalty Risk
values: Advising such a position may lead to penalties if not properly disclosed.
Advising such a position carries a significant risk of penalties for both the advisor and the taxpayer.

title: AICPA Statements on Standards for Tax Services
description: Understand the ethical standards CPAs must follow in tax practice

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The AICPA Statements on Standards for Tax Services (SSTS) set ethical guidelines specifically for CPAs providing tax services. While Circular 230 is law, SSTS represents professional standards that can also lead to AICPA discipline!

sections.title: Overview of SSTS
sections.content:
**What are SSTS?**

• Ethical standards for CPAs in tax practice
• Complement (don't replace) Circular 230
• Enforceable through AICPA ethics process
• Cover return preparation, positions, advice

**7 Statements covering tax practice**

sections.title: SSTS No. 1: Tax Return Positions
sections.content:
**Standard for taking positions:**

• Must have **good faith belief** position is warranted
• Position should have **realistic possibility of being sustained**
• Can take aggressive position if properly disclosed

**Key:** Don't sign returns with positions lacking reasonable basis

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: 7 SSTS Standards
sections.content:
**"PAPERS QE"** for 7 SSTS:

**P**ositions on returns (SSTS 1)
**A**nswers to questions (SSTS 2)
**P**rocedural aspects (SSTS 3)
**E**stimates (SSTS 4)
**R**ecommendations (SSTS 5) - omit
**S**ubsequent knowledge (SSTS 6)

**Q**uality of advice (SSTS 7)
**E**stimates again considered

sections.title: SSTS No. 2: Answers to Questions
sections.content:
**Regarding questions on returns:**

• CPA should make reasonable effort to obtain answers
• Can sign return with unanswered questions if:
  - Information not readily available
  - Answer unlikely to be significant

**Document reasons for omitting answers!**

sections.title: SSTS No. 3: Procedural Aspects
sections.content:
**Certain procedural matters:**

• CPA can rely on information from client without verification
• UNLESS information appears incorrect/incomplete
• Should make reasonable inquiries

**"Trust but verify" when things don't look right**

sections.title: SSTS No. 4: Use of Estimates
sections.content:
**When estimates are acceptable:**

• Records destroyed by fire/disaster
• Exact data not available
• Estimates don't imply greater accuracy

**Requirements:**
• Estimate must be reasonable
• Disclose if required by law
• Don't use if prohibited by tax law

sections.title: SSTS No. 6: Knowledge of Error
sections.content:
**When CPA discovers error:**

**Must:**
• Promptly inform client of error
• Advise client of potential consequences
• Recommend corrective action

**Cannot:**
• Disclose error to IRS without client consent
• Continue to represent if client refuses to correct material error

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Client Confidentiality
sections.content:
**CPA discovers prior year error:**

**CANNOT inform IRS directly!**

**Must:**
1. Tell client about error
2. Recommend filing amended return
3. Consider withdrawal if client refuses

**Client decides whether to correct—not the CPA**

(Unless subpoenaed or other legal exception)

sections.title: SSTS No. 7: Form and Content of Advice
sections.content:
**Tax advice should:**

• Reflect professional judgment
• Consider all relevant facts
• Comply with applicable standards
• Be documented appropriately

**Written advice for significant matters preferred**

**Must advise client of relevant tax return disclosure requirements**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SSTS are AICPA ethical standards for CPAs in tax
Tax positions need realistic possibility of success
Can rely on client info unless appears incorrect
Estimates OK when reasonable and disclosed
Must inform client of discovered errors
Cannot disclose errors to IRS without consent
May need to withdraw if client won't correct material errors

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A CPA has prepared a tax return that includes a deduction that, while aggressive, has a realistic possibility of being sustained on its merits if challenged. The deduction is not disclosed on the tax return. According to SSTS No. 1, Tax Return Positions, is this permissible?
options: No, the position must always be disclosed regardless of the possibility of being sustained.
Yes, the position is permissible because it has a realistic possibility of being sustained, even without disclosure.
No, the CPA must have a 'more likely than not' belief that the position will be sustained.
Yes, as long as the client approves of the aggressive position.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: SSTS No. 1 allows a CPA to take a position that has a realistic possibility of being sustained on its merits, even if it's an aggressive position, without disclosure. Disclosure is only required when the position lacks a reasonable basis.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the core requirement of 'realistic possibility' as a threshold.

reveal.question: What is the primary purpose of the AICPA Statements on Standards for Tax Services (SSTS)?
reveal.answer: To provide ethical standards for CPAs in tax practice, covering areas such as return preparation, positions taken on returns, and tax advice. These standards complement, but do not replace, Circular 230 and are enforceable through the AICPA's ethics process.

knowledgeCheck.question: A CPA is preparing a client's tax return and finds that the client is unable to provide an answer to a specific question on the return. The CPA believes the information is not readily available and that the answer is unlikely to significantly impact the tax liability. According to SSTS No. 2, what should the CPA do?
options: Leave the question blank and not sign the return.
Sign the return but only if the client provides a written statement explaining the missing information.
Sign the return and document the reasons for omitting the answer.
Estimate the answer to the question based on similar returns the CPA has prepared.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: SSTS No. 2 states that a CPA can sign a return with unanswered questions if the information is not readily available and the answer is unlikely to be significant. The CPA must document the reasons for omitting the answer.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Recall the conditions under which a CPA can sign a return with unanswered questions.

title: Tax Preparer Penalties
description: Learn the penalties that apply to tax return preparers

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Tax preparers face significant penalties for improper conduct! Understanding these penalties—including the dollar amounts and standards—is essential for REG. The exam loves testing the difference between negligence and willful violations!

sections.title: Who is a Tax Return Preparer?
sections.content:
**Tax return preparer =** Person who prepares for compensation any return or claim for refund

**Includes:**
• Signing preparers (primary responsibility)
• Non-signing preparers (provide substantial portion)

**Excludes:**
• Furnishing typing/clerical services
• Preparing return for no compensation
• Preparing return for employer (as employee)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Preparer Penalties
sections.content:
**"1-5 Rule"** for Section 6694:

**$1,000** = Unreasonable (negligent)
**$5,000** = Willful (intentional)

**OR percentage of fees:**
• 50% for unreasonable
• 75% for willful

**Willful = 5x the penalty!**

sections.title: Reasonable Cause Defense
sections.content:
**6694(a) penalty can be avoided if:**

• Reasonable cause for position
• Preparer acted in good faith

**Factors considered:**
• Reliance on information provided
• Nature of error
• Whether position was disclosed
• Preparer's normal office practices

sections.title: Position Standards
sections.content:
**Undisclosed positions require:**
• Substantial authority (more than 33%)

**Disclosed positions require:**
• Reasonable basis (approximately 20%+)

**Tax shelter positions require:**
• More likely than not (>50%)
• Reasonable belief position would be sustained

sections.title: Other Preparer Penalties
rows: Failure to furnish copy to taxpayer

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Due Diligence for Credits
sections.content:
**Special due diligence required for:**

• Earned Income Credit (EIC)
• Child Tax Credit
• American Opportunity Credit
• Head of Household status

**Must complete Form 8867 (due diligence checklist)**

**Penalty: $560 PER FAILURE**

**Return claiming all four = potential $2,240 penalty!**

sections.title: Section 6701: Aiding & Abetting
sections.content:
**Penalty for helping others understate tax:**

• **$1,000** per individual return
• **$10,000** per corporate return

**Applies when:**
• Aids in preparing false document
• Knows document would be used for tax purposes
• Knows document would result in understatement

sections.title: Injunctions Against Preparers
sections.content:
**IRS can seek court injunction to:**

• Prohibit preparer from continuing conduct
• Bar preparer from tax preparation business
• Require compliance with Circular 230

**For repeated violations or egregious conduct**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 6694(a): $1,000 or 50% of fee for unreasonable positions
6694(b): $5,000 or 75% of fee for willful/reckless conduct
Due diligence required for EIC, CTC, AOTC, HOH ($560 penalty)
Cannot negotiate client refund checks ($560 penalty)
$50 penalty for various administrative failures
Aiding understatement: $1,000 (individual) or $10,000 (corporate)
Disclosure and reasonable basis can reduce position penalty risk

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following individuals would NOT be considered a tax return preparer for purposes of IRC §6694?
options: A CPA who prepares a client's tax return for a fee.
A software company that designs tax preparation software used by taxpayers.
An enrolled agent who reviews a tax return prepared by a junior staff member and signs the return.

An individual who provides data entry services by inputting information into tax preparation software based on source documents provided by the taxpayer, and who receives compensation for their services.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A software company is providing a product, not preparing the return. Tax return preparers directly prepare returns for compensation. Note that both signing and nonsigning preparers are included, as long as they prepare a substantial portion of the return.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the level of involvement in the actual tax preparation process.

reveal.question: List three factors considered when determining if a tax preparer has reasonable cause for an incorrect position on a tax return and acted in good faith.
reveal.answer: 1. Reliance on information provided by the taxpayer. 2. The nature of the error. 3. Whether the position was disclosed on the tax return. 4. The preparer's normal office practices.

rows.label: Primary Responsibility
values: Yes
No, provides substantial portion

rows.label: Definition
values: Signs the tax return
Prepares a substantial portion of the return but does NOT sign it

rows.label: Subject to Penalties
values: Yes
Yes

title: Filing Requirements & Deadlines
description: Master individual and business tax filing deadlines

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Missing a tax deadline can be costly! Knowing when different returns are due—and how extensions work—is fundamental knowledge for any tax professional. These dates are definitely tested on REG!

sections.title: Individual Returns (Form 1040)
sections.content:
**Original due date:** April 15

**Automatic extension:** 6 months to October 15
• File Form 4868
• Extension is to FILE, not to PAY
• Must estimate and pay tax due by April 15

**If date falls on weekend/holiday:** Next business day

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Due Dates
sections.content:
**"Partnerships and S Corps are EARLY"**

**March 15:** Pass-through entities (S Corps, Partnerships)
**April 15:** Individuals, C Corps, Trusts
**May 15:** Exempt organizations

**Why early for pass-throughs?** So K-1s can be sent to owners for their April 15 returns!

sections.title: Fiscal Year Filers
sections.content:
**For fiscal year entities:**

Due date = 15th day of 3rd or 4th month after year-end

**Example:** June 30 fiscal year-end
• C Corp due: October 15 (4th month)
• S Corp due: September 15 (3rd month)
• Partnership due: September 15 (3rd month)

sections.title: Who Must File?
sections.content:
**Individuals must file if gross income exceeds:**

**2025 thresholds (approximate):**
• Single, under 65: $14,600
• Single, 65+: $16,550
• MFJ, both under 65: $29,200
• Head of Household: $21,900

**Must also file if:**
• Self-employment income ≥ $400
• Special taxes owed (AMT, household employee)
• Received advance premium tax credits

sections.title: Extension Rules
sections.content:
**Extension facts:**

• Automatic if properly filed by due date
• Must estimate tax liability
• Interest runs from original due date
• Failure-to-pay penalty still applies (reduced rate)
• Extension does NOT extend time to pay!

**Penalty relief:** First-time penalty abatement available

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Extension Doesn't Extend Payment
sections.content:
**Filing extension ≠ Payment extension!**

**With extension:**
• Interest accrues from April 15
• Failure-to-pay penalty applies (0.5%/month)
• BUT failure-to-file penalty avoided

**Failure-to-file (5%/month) > Failure-to-pay (0.5%/month)**

**If you can't pay, STILL FILE on time!**

sections.title: Estimated Tax Payments
sections.content:
**Quarterly due dates:**
• Q1: April 15
• Q2: June 15
• Q3: September 15
• Q4: January 15 (following year)

**Required if:**
• Expected tax liability ≥ $1,000
• Withholding/credits won't cover obligation

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Individuals/C Corps: April 15 (6-month extension)
S Corps/Partnerships: March 15 (6-month extension)
Extensions extend filing, NOT payment deadline
Interest runs from original due date regardless
Filing thresholds vary by filing status and age
Self-employment income ≥ $400 requires filing
Estimated payments due quarterly (4/15, 6/15, 9/15, 1/15)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: John filed Form 4868 on April 10, 2024, to obtain an automatic extension for filing his 2023 individual income tax return. He estimated his tax liability to be $5,000 but only remitted $3,000 with Form 4868. He filed his return on October 10, 2024, showing an actual tax liability of $6,000. Regarding penalties, which of the following is TRUE?
options: John is only subject to penalties on the underpaid tax if the amount is greater than 10% of the tax required to be shown on the return.
John is only subject to penalties on the underpaid tax if the amount is greater than $500.
John is subject to penalties for both failure to file and failure to pay.
John is subject to a penalty for failure to pay, but not failure to file, as he filed for an extension.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The extension to file does not extend the time to pay. Since John underpaid his tax liability by $3,000 ($6,000 actual - $3,000 paid), he is subject to a failure-to-pay penalty. Since he properly filed Form 4868, he avoided the failure-to-file penalty.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that extensions are only for *filing*, not for *paying*.

reveal.question: What is the general rule for determining the due date of a fiscal year entity's tax return?
reveal.answer: The due date is generally the 15th day of the 3rd or 4th month after the entity's fiscal year-end. C-Corps are generally the 4th month, while S-Corps and Partnerships are generally the 3rd month.

sections.title: Comparing C Corp, S Corp, and Partnership Fiscal Year Filing Deadlines

rows.label: Fiscal Year-End June 30
values: October 15
September 15
September 15

rows.label: Fiscal Year-End January 31
values: May 15
April 15
April 15

title: Statute of Limitations: Assessment & Collection
description: Understand time limits for IRS assessment and collection actions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The statute of limitations protects taxpayers from perpetual IRS scrutiny—but there are critical exceptions! Knowing when the IRS can still assess tax or collect is essential. The exam frequently tests the 3-year vs 6-year vs unlimited rules!

sections.title: General Assessment Period
sections.content:
**Standard rule: 3 years**

• IRS has 3 years from later of:
  - Due date of return (with extensions), or
  - Date return was actually filed

• After 3 years, IRS cannot assess additional tax

**Return filed early?** Clock starts on due date, not filing date

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Assessment SOL
sections.content:
**"3-6-∞"** Rule:

**3** years = Normal returns
**6** years = >25% gross income omitted
**∞** (Unlimited) = Fraud or no return

**"If you cheat or don't file, they can come after you FOREVER!"**

sections.title: 25% Gross Income Omission
sections.content:
**6-year rule applies when:**

• Taxpayer omits >25% of gross income
• Gross income = total receipts before deductions

**Example:**
• Reported gross income: $80,000
• Actual gross income: $120,000
• Omission: $40,000 (50% of reported)
• 6-year rule applies

**Note:** Adequate disclosure can prevent 6-year rule

sections.title: Collection Statute
sections.content:
**Collection period: 10 years**

• IRS has 10 years from assessment date to collect
• Period can be extended by:
  - Bankruptcy filing
  - Installment agreement request
  - Collection due process hearing
  - Offer in compromise

**Assessment ≠ Collection**
• Assessment = Determining you owe
• Collection = Actually getting the money

sections.title: Suspensions and Extensions
sections.content:
**SOL can be suspended during:**

• IRS audit when 90-day letter issued
• Bankruptcy proceedings
• Taxpayer outside US for 6+ months
• Third-party summons enforcement
• Installment agreement request

**Consent:** Taxpayer can agree to extend assessment period (Form 872)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: When Does Clock Start?
sections.content:
**Clock starts on LATER of:**

• Due date (including extensions), OR
• Actual filing date

**Example:**
• Return due April 15, 2024
• Filed March 1, 2024 (early)
• SOL runs from April 15, 2024

**Example 2:**
• Return due April 15, 2024
• Filed August 1, 2024 (late)
• SOL runs from August 1, 2024

sections.title: Refund Claims
sections.content:
**Taxpayer must claim refund within:**

• **3 years from filing date**, OR
• **2 years from payment date**
• Whichever is LATER

**Refund limited to:**
• If 3-year rule: Tax paid in 3 years + extensions
• If 2-year rule: Tax paid in 2 years

**Miss the deadline = No refund!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Normal assessment period: 3 years from filing
25%+ gross income omission: 6 years
Fraud or no return filed: Unlimited
Collection period: 10 years from assessment
Early-filed returns: Clock starts on due date
Refund claims: 3 years from filing or 2 years from payment
SOL can be suspended or extended by agreement

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: John filed his 2022 tax return on March 15, 2023. The due date for the return, including a valid extension, was October 15, 2023. What is the last day the IRS can assess additional tax for John's 2022 tax year, assuming no fraud or substantial omission of income?
options: March 15, 2026
April 15, 2026
October 15, 2026
December 31, 2026
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The IRS has 3 years from the *later* of the due date with extensions or the date the return was filed. In this case, the later date is October 15, 2023. Therefore, the IRS has until October 15, 2026, to assess additional tax.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to consider both the filing date and the extended due date. Which one is later?

reveal.question: What specific condition triggers the extended 6-year statute of limitations for assessment?
reveal.answer: The 6-year statute of limitations is triggered when a taxpayer omits more than 25% of their *gross income* from their tax return. Adequate disclosure of the omitted income can prevent the application of the 6-year rule.

knowledgeCheck.question: Susan reported gross income of $150,000 on her tax return. Upon audit, the IRS determined her actual gross income was $220,000. Does the 6-year statute of limitations apply to Susan's return?
options: Yes, because the omission is greater than $25,000.
No, because the omission is less than 25% of the reported gross income.
Yes, because the omission is greater than 25% of the reported gross income.
No, because the omission is less than 25% of the actual gross income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The omission is $70,000 ($220,000 - $150,000). 25% of the *reported* gross income is $37,500 (25% x $150,000). Since the omission ($70,000) is greater than 25% of the reported gross income ($37,500), the 6-year statute of limitations applies.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate the amount of the omission. Then calculate 25% of the *reported* gross income. Compare the two amounts.

title: IRS Audit Process & Appeals
description: Navigate the IRS examination and appeals procedures

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Understanding the IRS audit process helps you advise clients and navigate disputes! From initial contact through Tax Court, knowing the procedures, deadlines, and taxpayer rights is critical for tax practice and the REG exam!

sections.title: Types of IRS Examinations
sections.content:
**Three types:**

**1. Correspondence Audit**
• Conducted by mail
• Usually single-issue (W-2 match, etc.)
• Most common type

**2. Office Audit**
• At IRS office
• More complex issues

**3. Field Audit**
• At taxpayer's location
• Business/high-income returns
• Most comprehensive

sections.title: The Examination Process
sections.content:
**Steps:**

1. **Selection** - Return flagged (DIF score, info matching)
2. **Initial Contact** - IRS sends notice requesting documents
3. **Examination** - Review of records, interviews
4. **Findings** - Examiner proposes adjustments
5. **Agreement/Disagreement** - Sign or appeal

**Taxpayer can have representative present (POA)**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Audit Sequence
sections.content:
**"SERF"** for audit outcomes:

**S**ign and agree (pay immediately)
**E**xaminer's findings (receive report)
**R**equest appeals conference
**F**ile petition in court (if needed)

**Always try Appeals before Court!**

sections.title: The 30-Day Letter
sections.content:
**Preliminary notice of deficiency:**

• Sent when examiner proposes adjustments
• Includes examination report
• Taxpayer has 30 days to:
  - Agree and pay
  - Request Appeals conference
  - Do nothing (triggers 90-day letter)

**Not required by law—but standard practice**

sections.title: The 90-Day Letter (Statutory Notice)
sections.content:
**Statutory Notice of Deficiency:**

• Official notice of proposed tax due
• MUST be sent before IRS can assess
• Taxpayer has 90 days (150 if abroad) to:
  - File petition in Tax Court, OR
  - Do nothing (tax becomes assessable)

**This is a "ticket to Tax Court"**

sections.title: IRS Appeals
sections.content:
**Appeals Conference:**

• Independent review within IRS
• Settlement authority (hazards of litigation)
• Can resolve 85%+ of cases
• No new issues raised
• Written protest required if >$25,000

**Advantages:**
• Less formal than court
• Faster resolution
• No legal fees

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Tax Court vs Other Courts
sections.content:
**Tax Court:**
• Don't have to pay first!
• File within 90 days of notice
• Tax experts (no jury)

**District Court or Court of Federal Claims:**
• Must PAY FIRST, then sue for refund
• Jury trial available (District Court only)

**Want to fight without paying? Tax Court!**

sections.title: Taxpayer Rights
sections.content:
**Taxpayer Bill of Rights includes:**

• Right to be informed
• Right to quality service
• Right to pay no more than correct amount
• Right to challenge IRS position
• Right to appeal
• Right to finality
• Right to privacy
• Right to confidentiality
• Right to representation
• Right to a fair tax system

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Three audit types: Correspondence, Office, Field
30-day letter = Preliminary (can request Appeals)
90-day letter = Statutory notice (ticket to Tax Court)
Tax Court: No payment required before petition
District Court/Claims Court: Must pay first, then sue for refund
Appeals can settle most cases without litigation
Written protest required for appeals if >$25,000

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which type of IRS audit is MOST likely to be conducted at the taxpayer's place of business and involve a comprehensive review of their business and high-income tax return?
options: Correspondence Audit
Office Audit
Field Audit
Desk Audit
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A Field Audit is conducted at the taxpayer's location (often their business) and is the most comprehensive, typically involving business or high-income returns. Correspondence Audits are done by mail for single issues, and Office Audits are conducted at an IRS office for more complex issues.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which audit type involves the most in-depth review and physical presence.

reveal.question: After receiving a 30-day letter, what are the taxpayer's three options?
reveal.answer: 1. Agree to the proposed adjustments and pay the additional tax. 2. Request an Appeals conference with the IRS. 3. Do nothing, which will result in the issuance of a 90-day letter.

rows.label: Location
values: IRS Office
Taxpayer's Location

rows.label: Complexity
values: More complex than Correspondence Audit
Most comprehensive

rows.label: Typical Returns
values: Varies, generally more complex than single-issue audits.
Business/High-Income Returns

title: Taxpayer Penalties: Civil & Criminal
description: Understand the penalties taxpayers face for noncompliance

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Taxpayer penalties can be devastating—from modest late-filing fees to criminal prosecution! Understanding the different penalty tiers and when they apply helps you advise clients and avoid problems. This is heavily tested material!

sections.title: Failure-to-File Penalty
sections.content:
**Section 6651(a)(1):**

• **5% per month** (or fraction) of tax due
• Maximum: **25%** of tax due
• Reduced to 4.5% when failure-to-pay also applies
• Minimum penalty if >60 days late: Lesser of $485 or 100% of tax

**Reasonable cause defense available**

sections.title: Failure-to-Pay Penalty
sections.content:
**Section 6651(a)(2):**

• **0.5% per month** of unpaid tax
• Maximum: **25%** of unpaid tax
• Increases to 1% if IRS issues notice of levy
• Reduced to 0.25% if on installment plan

**Runs from due date until paid**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: File vs Pay Penalties
sections.content:
**"5-point-5 Rule"**

**Fail to FILE: 5%**/month (max 25%)
**Fail to PAY: 0.5%**/month (max 25%)

**File penalty is 10x higher!**

**Message:** Even if you can't pay, FILE on time to avoid the bigger penalty!

sections.title: Substantial Understatement
sections.content:
**Understatement is substantial if:**

**Individuals:**
• Greater of $5,000 OR 10% of tax shown

**Corporations:**
• Greater of $10,000 OR 10% of tax (or $10M)

**Reduction:** Understatement reduced by:
• Positions with substantial authority
• Adequately disclosed positions with reasonable basis

sections.title: Fraud Penalty
sections.content:
**Section 6663 - Civil Fraud:**

• **75% of underpayment** due to fraud
• IRS must prove fraud by clear and convincing evidence
• Once fraud shown, ALL underpayment presumed fraudulent
• Taxpayer must then prove non-fraud portions

**No statute of limitations for fraud!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Penalty Stacking
sections.content:
**Penalties that DON'T stack:**

• Fraud (75%) and accuracy-related (20%) are MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE
• If fraud applies, accuracy-related does not

**Penalties that DO stack:**
• Failure-to-file + Failure-to-pay (but combined max is 5%/month)
• Interest runs on top of all penalties

**Fraud = 75% (not 95% combining them!)**

sections.title: Criminal Penalties
sections.content:
**Criminal tax violations:**

• **Tax evasion (§7201):** Up to $100,000 ($500K corp) + 5 years
• **Willful failure to file (§7203):** Up to $25,000 + 1 year
• **Filing false return (§7206):** Up to $100,000 + 3 years

**Require WILLFUL intent**—must prove beyond reasonable doubt

**Criminal ≠ Civil—can face both!**

sections.title: Estimated Tax Penalty
sections.content:
**Underpayment of estimated tax:**

• Interest-based penalty (not percentage)
• No penalty if:
  - Tax due < $1,000
  - Paid 90% of current year, OR
  - Paid 100% of prior year (110% if AGI >$150K)

**Safe harbor:** Pay 100%/110% of prior year

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Failure-to-file: 5%/month (max 25%)
Failure-to-pay: 0.5%/month (max 25%)
Accuracy-related: 20% (negligence, substantial understatement)
Fraud: 75% of underpayment (no SOL)
Substantial understatement: >$5,000 or >10% of tax
Fraud and accuracy penalties don't stack
Criminal penalties require willful intent

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: John filed his tax return 75 days after the due date, without a valid extension. The tax due on his return was $5,000. Ignoring any potential failure-to-pay penalty and assuming no reasonable cause exists, what is the amount of the failure-to-file penalty John will face?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The failure-to-file penalty is 5% per month (or fraction thereof) up to a maximum of 25%. Since John filed 75 days late, this is more than 60 days, triggering the minimum penalty. The minimum penalty is the lesser of $485 or 100% of the tax due. 100% of the tax due is $5,000. Therefore, the penalty is $485.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to check for the minimum penalty when returns are filed more than 60 days late!

reveal.question: What is the standard monthly failure-to-pay penalty rate?
reveal.answer: The standard monthly failure-to-pay penalty rate is 0.5% of the unpaid tax.

rows.label: Monthly Penalty Rate
values: 5% (or 4.5% if failure-to-pay also applies)
0.5% (or 1% after levy notice, 0.25% on installment plan)

rows.label: Maximum Penalty
values: 25% of tax due
25% of unpaid tax

rows.label: Trigger
values: Filing late
Not paying on time

title: Power of Attorney & Authorizations
description: Learn how representatives are authorized to act before the IRS

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Before you can represent a client before the IRS, proper authorization must be in place! Understanding the different IRS forms and authorization levels is essential for tax practice. The exam tests the differences between POA and other authorizations!

sections.title: Form 2848: Power of Attorney
sections.content:
**Power of Attorney and Declaration of Representative:**

• Authorizes representative to act on taxpayer's behalf
• Can receive confidential information
• Can sign documents, attend meetings, negotiate
• Must specify tax matters and years covered
• Representative must be authorized to practice (CPA, attorney, EA)

sections.title: POA Authorization Levels
sections.content:
**Form 2848 authorizes representative to:**

• Receive and inspect confidential tax information
• Perform acts on taxpayer's behalf
• Sign consents to extend statute
• Execute waivers
• Sign closing agreements (if specifically authorized)
• Receive refund checks? NO (unless specifically authorized)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: IRS Authorization Forms
sections.content:
**"2-8-86"** for authorization forms:

**2848** = Power of Attorney (full representation)
**8821** = Tax Information Authorization (view only)
**86**21 = Third Party Designee (limited)

**2848 > 8821 > Designee**

sections.title: Form 8821: Tax Information Authorization
sections.content:
**Tax Information Authorization:**

• Authorizes disclosure of tax info to designee
• Does NOT allow representation before IRS
• Can receive notices and transcripts
• Cannot sign documents or negotiate

**Use when:** Third party (like mortgage company) needs tax info

sections.title: Third Party Designee (Checkbox)
sections.content:
**Checkbox on return:**

• Limited authorization on tax return
• Allows IRS to discuss return with preparer
• Only covers that specific return
• Valid until due date of following year's return

**Simple authorization for return-related questions only**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Who Can Represent
sections.content:
**Only authorized practitioners can represent:**

• Attorneys
• CPAs
• Enrolled Agents

**Form 8821 can name ANYONE** (bank, family member)

**But Form 2848 must name authorized practitioner!**

**Exception:** Limited practice rights for certain preparers on returns they prepared

sections.title: Centralized Authorization File (CAF)
sections.content:
**IRS maintains CAF system:**

• Records all active POAs and authorizations
• Each representative has CAF number
• IRS sends copies of notices to authorized representatives
• Authorizations remain until revoked or superseded

**CAF number is permanent identifier for practitioner**

sections.title: Revoking Authorization
sections.content:
**To revoke:**

• Written statement to IRS
• New POA for same matters supersedes prior
• Death of taxpayer terminates POA
• Representative can withdraw

**Multiple representatives:** Can name more than one—each acts independently unless specified

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Form 2848: Full POA for authorized practitioners only
Form 8821: Tax info disclosure (no representation)
Third party designee: Limited, return-specific authorization
Only CPAs, attorneys, EAs can be named on Form 2848
Anyone can be named on Form 8821
POA must specify tax matters and periods covered
CAF system tracks active authorizations

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following actions requires specific authorization on Form 2848, Power of Attorney and Declaration of Representative?
options: Receiving confidential tax information.
Performing acts on the taxpayer's behalf.
Receiving refund checks on behalf of the taxpayer.
Signing consents to extend the statute of limitations.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While Form 2848 grants broad authority to represent a taxpayer, receiving refund checks requires specific authorization. The other options are generally covered by the standard authorization.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which actions represent the *most* direct control over the taxpayer's assets.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Authorizes representation and access to confidential tax information.
Authorizes disclosure of tax information to a designated third party.

rows.label: Representation Before IRS
values: Yes, representative can act on taxpayer's behalf.
No, designee cannot represent the taxpayer.

rows.label: Signing Documents
values: Yes, representative can sign documents (with some exceptions).
No, designee cannot sign documents.

rows.label: Negotiation
values: Yes, representative can negotiate with the IRS.
No, designee cannot negotiate with the IRS.

reveal.question: True or False: Form 8821 allows a mortgage company to represent a taxpayer in an audit.
reveal.answer: False. Form 8821 only authorizes the disclosure of tax information. It does NOT grant representation rights.

title: Agency: Formation & Authority
description: Understand how agency relationships are created and the types of authority

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Agency law is foundational to business! Every time someone acts on behalf of another—employees, partners, corporate officers—agency principles apply. Understanding how authority is created and when principals are bound is essential for REG!

sections.title: What is Agency?
sections.content:
**Agency = Fiduciary relationship**

• **Principal:** Party authorizing action
• **Agent:** Party acting on principal's behalf
• **Third Party:** Outsider dealing with agent

**Key question:** When does the agent's action bind the principal?

sections.title: Creating Agency Relationships
sections.content:
**Agency can be created by:**

1. **Express agreement** - Written or oral
2. **Implied conduct** - Actions showing intent
3. **Estoppel** - Principal's conduct creates appearance
4. **Ratification** - Principal adopts unauthorized act
5. **Operation of law** - Emergency situations

**No consideration required!**

sections.title: Types of Authority
rows: Reasonably necessary to carry out express
Store clerk appearing to have sales authority
VP of Sales binding company to contracts

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Types of Authority
sections.content:
**"EAIR"** the types:

**E**xpress - Principal SAID it
**A**pparent - Third party BELIEVED it
**I**mplied - Reasonably NECESSARY
**R**atification - Principal ADOPTED it after

**"Did the principal give authority or did it appear they did?"**

sections.title: Apparent Authority
sections.content:
**Created by principal's conduct:**

• Principal must manifest to third party
• Third party must reasonably rely
• Agent's own statements NOT enough!

**Example:**
• Company gives employee business cards with "Sales Manager" title
• Customer reasonably believes manager can negotiate contracts
• Company bound even if manager exceeded actual authority

sections.title: Ratification
sections.content:
**Adopting an unauthorized act:**

**Requirements:**
• Agent purported to act for principal
• Principal knew material facts
• Principal accepted benefits OR affirmed transaction
• Principal had capacity at time of ratification

**Effect:** Retroactive—as if originally authorized

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Agent's Own Statements
sections.content:
**Agent cannot create own apparent authority!**

**Wrong:** Agent tells customer "I have authority to offer 50% discount"
→ This alone doesn't bind principal

**Right:** Principal tells customer "This agent handles all discounts"
→ Creates apparent authority

**Authority must come FROM principal, not agent!**

sections.title: Termination of Agency
sections.content:
**By act of parties:**
• Mutual agreement
• Revocation by principal
• Renunciation by agent
• Accomplishment of purpose
• Expiration of time

**By operation of law:**
• Death of principal or agent
• Incapacity of principal
• Bankruptcy
• Illegality of subject matter

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Agency is a fiduciary relationship requiring consent, not consideration
Express authority: Explicitly granted
Implied authority: Reasonably necessary to carry out express
Apparent authority: Created by principal's conduct toward third parties
Agent's own statements don't create apparent authority
Ratification makes unauthorized acts retroactively authorized
Death or incapacity of principal terminates agency

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Pamela owns a retail store. She overhears her employee, Dwight, telling a customer that he is authorized to negotiate a bulk discount of 15% on purchases over $1,000. Pamela does not correct Dwight, even though he is not actually authorized to offer such a discount. The customer, relying on Dwight's representation, purchases $1,500 worth of goods. Is Pamela bound by Dwight's promise of a 15% discount?
options: No, because Dwight did not have actual authority to offer the discount.
Yes, because Dwight had express authority to offer the discount.
Yes, because Dwight had apparent authority to offer the discount due to Pamela's inaction.
No, because bulk discounts must be in writing to be enforceable.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Pamela is bound by Dwight's promise under the doctrine of estoppel. By failing to correct Dwight's misrepresentation, Pamela created the appearance that Dwight had the authority to offer the discount, leading the customer to reasonably rely on that representation to their detriment.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the different ways agency can be created, and how the principal's conduct affects the agent's authority.

sections.title: Recall the Types of Agency Creation

reveal.question: List the five ways an agency relationship can be created:
reveal.answer:
1. Express agreement
2. Implied conduct
3. Estoppel
4. Ratification
5. Operation of law

rows.label: Definition
values: Authority specifically delegated to the agent by the principal, either orally or in writing.
Authority that is reasonably inferred from the principal's words, conduct, or the circumstances.

rows.label: Source
values: Direct communication from principal.
Inferred from the agent's position or prior dealings.

rows.label: Example
values: A principal explicitly tells an agent to sign a contract on their behalf.
A store manager has the implied authority to hire and fire employees.

title: Agency: Duties & Liabilities
description: Learn the duties agents and principals owe each other and to third parties

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Agency creates fiduciary duties—some of the highest obligations in law! When agents breach these duties, or when principals are held liable for agent acts, major consequences follow. Understanding who's liable for what is exam gold!

sections.title: Agent's Duties to Principal
content: **Duty of Loyalty** - Act in principal's best interest, no self-dealing
**Duty of Obedience** - Follow reasonable instructions
**Duty of Care** - Exercise reasonable skill and care
**Duty to Account** - Maintain records, turn over money/property
**Duty to Notify** - Inform principal of relevant information
**Duty of Confidentiality** - Protect principal's confidential information

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Agent Duties
sections.content:
**"LOCA-NC"** for agent duties:

**L**oyalty
**O**bedience
**C**are
**A**ccount
**N**otify
**C**onfidentiality

**Agents must be LOYAL and CAREFUL!**

sections.title: Principal's Duties to Agent
sections.content:
**Principal must:**

• **Compensate** agent as agreed
• **Reimburse** authorized expenses
• **Indemnify** for losses from authorized acts
• **Cooperate** and not hinder agent's performance

**These duties can be modified by agreement**

sections.title: Disclosed vs Undisclosed Principal
sections.content:
**Disclosed Principal:**
• Third party knows principal exists AND identity
• Agent typically not personally liable

**Partially Disclosed:**
• Third party knows principal exists but NOT identity
• BOTH agent and principal liable

**Undisclosed:**
• Third party doesn't know agent acts for principal
• Agent personally liable; principal liable when revealed

sections.title: Liability for Torts
sections.content:
**Respondeat Superior:**

• Principal liable for agent's torts
• **Only if:** Committed within scope of employment
• Agent is ALSO personally liable

**Scope of employment factors:**
• Authorized time and place?
• Furthering employer's business?
• Using employer's tools/methods?

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Independent Contractors
sections.content:
**Principal generally NOT liable for IC torts!**

**Why?** Less control over HOW work is done

**Exceptions (principal IS liable):**
• Inherently dangerous activities
• Non-delegable duties
• Principal was negligent in hiring
• Principal controlled the work

**Employee vs IC = Control over manner of work**

sections.title: Frolic vs Detour
sections.content:
**Was agent within scope of employment?**

**Detour:** Minor deviation from duties
→ Still within scope, principal liable

**Frolic:** Major departure for personal reasons
→ Outside scope, principal NOT liable

**Example:**
• Quick stop for coffee = Detour (liable)
• Driving 50 miles to see friend = Frolic (not liable)

sections.title: Agent Exceeding Authority
sections.content:
**If agent exceeds authority:**

• Principal not bound to transaction
• UNLESS ratified or apparent authority exists
• Agent may be liable to:
  - Principal (breach of duty)
  - Third party (breach of implied warranty of authority)

**Agent impliedly warrants they have authority to act**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Agents owe fiduciary duties: Loyalty, Obedience, Care, Account, Notify
Disclosed principal: Agent generally not liable on contracts
Undisclosed principal: Agent personally liable
Respondeat superior: Principal liable for employee torts in scope
Independent contractor torts: Principal generally NOT liable
Frolic = Outside scope; Detour = Within scope
Agent warrants authority; liable if no authority exists

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: An agent, acting within their authorized scope, incurs a financial loss due to a lawsuit resulting from following the principal's instructions. Which duty requires the principal to cover the agent's loss?
options: Duty of Compensation
Duty of Reimbursement
Duty of Indemnification
Duty of Cooperation
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The duty of indemnification requires the principal to protect the agent from losses or liabilities incurred while acting on the principal's behalf and within the scope of their authority. Compensation covers agreed-upon payment, reimbursement covers expenses, and cooperation ensures the agent can perform their duties effectively.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which duty specifically protects the agent from negative financial consequences arising from their actions on behalf of the principal.

reveal.question: Name the four primary duties a principal owes to an agent.
reveal.answer:
1. Compensate the agent as agreed.
2. Reimburse the agent for authorized expenses.
3. Indemnify the agent for losses from authorized acts.
4. Cooperate with the agent and not hinder their performance.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding the duties a principal owes to an agent?
options: These duties are absolute and cannot be altered under any circumstances.
These duties are statutory and only state laws can modify them.
These duties are implied by law and can be modified by mutual agreement between the principal and agent.
These duties are only applicable in the absence of a written agency agreement.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The duties a principal owes to an agent are implied by law but can be modified by agreement between the parties. This allows flexibility in the agency relationship based on the specific needs and circumstances.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the principle of freedom of contract.

title: Contracts: Formation & Enforceability
description: Master the elements required to form a valid, enforceable contract

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Contracts are the backbone of business! Understanding when a contract exists and when it's enforceable is fundamental legal knowledge. The REG exam heavily tests offer, acceptance, consideration, and the Statute of Frauds!

sections.title: Elements of a Valid Contract
sections.content:
**All required for valid contract:**

1. **Offer** - Promise with definite terms
2. **Acceptance** - Agreement to offer terms
3. **Consideration** - Bargained-for exchange
4. **Capacity** - Legal ability to contract
5. **Legality** - Lawful purpose

**Missing any = No contract!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Contract Elements
sections.content:
**"OACCL"** (Oak-ul) for elements:

**O**ffer
**A**cceptance
**C**onsideration
**C**apacity
**L**egality

**"OAK trees make contracts LEGAL!"**

sections.title: The Offer
sections.content:
**Valid offer requires:**

• **Intent** - Objective manifestation of willingness
• **Definite terms** - Essential terms must be clear
• **Communication** - Must reach offeree

**NOT offers:**
• Advertisements (generally invitations to deal)
• Preliminary negotiations
• Statements of opinion
• Jokes (if reasonable person wouldn't take seriously)

sections.title: Termination of Offer
content: **Revocation** - Offeror withdraws (effective when received)
**Rejection** - Offeree declines (effective when received)
**Counteroffer** - New offer that rejects original
**Lapse of time** - Reasonable time or stated deadline
**Death/incapacity** - Of either party before acceptance
**Destruction** - Of subject matter
**Illegality** - Subject matter becomes illegal

sections.title: Acceptance
sections.content:
**Rules for acceptance:**

• Must be unequivocal (mirror image rule)
• Must be communicated to offeror
• Silence generally NOT acceptance
• Acceptance effective when SENT (mailbox rule)
• Revocation effective when RECEIVED

**UCC more flexible:** Additional terms may be included (between merchants)

sections.title: Consideration
sections.content:
**Bargained-for exchange:**

• Legal detriment OR benefit
• Must be bargained for
• Must be legally sufficient

**NOT valid consideration:**
• Past consideration (already done)
• Pre-existing duty (already obligated)
• Illusory promise (no real commitment)
• Gift promises

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Pre-existing Duty
sections.content:
**Promising to do what you're ALREADY obligated to do = No consideration!**

**Example:**
• Contractor agreed to build for $100K
• Contractor demands $20K more mid-project
• Owner agrees under pressure
• Modification NOT enforceable (no new consideration)

**Exception:** UCC allows good faith modifications without consideration

sections.title: Statute of Frauds
sections.content:
**Must be IN WRITING:**

**"MY LEGS"**
• **M**arriage promises
• **Y**ear+ contracts (can't perform within 1 year)
• **L**and/real estate contracts
• **E**xecutor promises (pay from own funds)
• **G**oods $500+ (UCC)
• **S**urety (guarantee another's debt)

**Writing must contain essential terms + signature**

sections.title: Capacity to Contract
sections.content:
**Lack of capacity:**

• **Minors** - Voidable by minor
• **Mental incapacity** - Voidable if other party knew
• **Intoxication** - Voidable if other party knew

**Voidable = Valid until disaffirmed**

**Minors liable for NECESSITIES (food, shelter, medical)**

sections.title: Defenses to Enforcement
rows: Can be disaffirmed by protected party

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Valid contract requires: Offer, Acceptance, Consideration, Capacity, Legality
Offer terminates on revocation, rejection, counteroffer, or lapse
Acceptance must mirror offer (common law)
Past performance is NOT valid consideration
Statute of Frauds: MY LEGS must be in writing
Minors' contracts are voidable (except necessities)
Void = Never existed; Voidable = Valid until disaffirmed

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following elements is NOT required for the formation of a valid and enforceable contract?
options: A) Offer
B) Acceptance
C) Written Documentation
D) Consideration
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A valid contract requires offer, acceptance, consideration, capacity, and legality. While written documentation can be helpful for proving the existence and terms of a contract, it is not always a required element for contract formation. Some contracts can be oral and still be enforceable, depending on the jurisdiction and subject matter (e.g., the Statute of Frauds requires certain contracts to be in writing).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about contracts you make every day. Are they always in writing?

reveal.question: List the three requirements for a valid offer.
reveal.answer:
1. Intent: Objective manifestation of willingness to enter into a contract.
2. Definite Terms: The essential terms of the offer must be clear and certain.
3. Communication: The offer must be communicated to the offeree.

knowledgeCheck.question: Ace Advertising publishes a weekly flyer advertising various products at discounted prices. One item listed is a high-definition television for $200, regularly priced at $800. Bob sees the advertisement and goes to Ace Advertising to purchase the television. Ace Advertising refuses to sell Bob the television at the advertised price, stating that they only had a limited quantity and those have already been sold. Is Ace Advertising obligated to sell Bob the television at the advertised price?
options: A) Yes, because the advertisement constitutes a valid offer which Bob accepted.
B) Yes, because Ace Advertising is obligated to honor its advertised prices.
C) No, because advertisements are generally considered invitations to deal, not offers.
D) No, unless Bob can prove that Ace Advertising intentionally misled him.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Advertisements are generally considered invitations to deal or invitations to negotiate, rather than offers. Therefore, Ace Advertising is not obligated to sell Bob the television at the advertised price. An offer requires a manifestation of intent to be bound, definite terms, and communication to the offeree. Advertisements typically lack the requisite intent to be bound to anyone who 'accepts' the 'offer'.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider what would happen if a store *had* to sell to everyone who saw an ad, regardless of stock.

rows.label: Intent
values: Objective manifestation of willingness to be bound.
Discussion or exploration of potential terms; no intent to be immediately bound.

rows.label: Definiteness
values: Contains definite and certain terms.
Terms may be vague or incomplete.

rows.label: Legal Effect
values: Creates the power of acceptance in the offeree.
Does not create the power of acceptance.

title: Contracts: Performance & Remedies
description: Understand contract performance standards and remedies for breach

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: What happens when contracts aren't fully performed? Understanding performance standards, breach, and available remedies is crucial. The exam tests the difference between complete, substantial, and material breach—and what remedies apply!

sections.title: Levels of Performance
rows: Other party must perform, minus damages

sections.title: Substantial Performance
sections.content:
**When performance is substantial:**

• Performing party acted in good faith
• Defects are minor and not willful
• Essential purpose of contract achieved

**Effect:**
• Other party must pay contract price MINUS cost to remedy defects

**Does NOT apply to sale of goods (UCC requires perfect tender)**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Performance Levels
sections.content:
**"CSM"** - Complete, Substantial, Material:

**C**omplete = You're done, get paid in full
**S**ubstantial = Minor issues, get paid minus fixes
**M**aterial = Major breach, other party walks away

**The more complete, the more you get!**

sections.title: Anticipatory Breach
sections.content:
**Before performance is due:**

• Party clearly indicates won't perform
• Must be unequivocal repudiation
• Non-breaching party can:
  - Sue immediately for breach
  - Wait for actual breach
  - Treat as material breach

**Can be retracted before other party relies on it**

sections.title: Damages: Compensatory
sections.content:
**Goal: Put non-breaching party in position if contract performed**

**Types:**
• **Expectation damages** - Benefit of the bargain
• **Direct damages** - Naturally flow from breach
• **Incidental damages** - Costs to find substitute
• **Consequential damages** - Special damages if foreseeable

**Must mitigate damages!**

sections.title: Other Damage Types
sections.content:
**Liquidated Damages:**
• Pre-agreed amount in contract
• Valid if: (1) Actual damages hard to estimate, (2) Amount reasonable
• If excessive = Unenforceable penalty

**Nominal Damages:**
• Small amount when breach proven but no actual loss
• Usually $1

**Punitive Damages:**
• Generally NOT available in contract (only tort)

sections.title: Equitable Remedies
rows: Unique goods/real estate; money damages inadequate
Correct written contract to match intent

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Specific Performance
sections.content:
**Specific performance (forcing performance) available when:**

✓ **Real estate** - Each parcel is unique
✓ **Unique goods** - Rare art, antiques
✗ **Personal services** - NEVER (13th Amendment)
✗ **Ordinary goods** - Money damages adequate

**Court won't force someone to work for you!**

sections.title: Duty to Mitigate
sections.content:
**Non-breaching party must minimize damages:**

• Take reasonable steps to reduce loss
• Cannot recover damages that could have been avoided
• Must not "pile up" damages by inaction

**Example:**
• Employee wrongfully fired
• Must seek comparable employment
• Damages reduced by what could have been earned

sections.title: Excuses for Nonperformance
content: **Impossibility** - Performance literally impossible (death, destruction)
**Impracticability** - Extreme difficulty beyond contemplation
**Frustration of Purpose** - Purpose destroyed by unforeseen event
**Mutual Rescission** - Both parties agree to cancel
**Accord and Satisfaction** - New agreement to settle existing dispute

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Substantial performance: Minor defects don't excuse payment
Material breach: Major failure discharges other party
Anticipatory breach: Can sue before performance due
Compensatory damages: Put in position as if performed
Specific performance: Only for unique goods/real estate
No specific performance for personal services
Non-breaching party must mitigate damages

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Builder Bob contracted with Owner Ollie to construct a storage shed for $10,000. Bob completed the shed, but used slightly thinner gauge metal than specified in the contract, resulting in a cost of $500 to replace the metal with the specified gauge. Bob acted in good faith and the shed is fully functional. Which of the following is correct?
options: Ollie owes Bob nothing because Bob did not perfectly perform the contract.
Ollie owes Bob $10,000 because Bob substantially performed the contract.
Ollie owes Bob $9,500 because Bob substantially performed the contract, but Ollie can deduct the cost to remedy the defect.
Ollie can sue Bob for breach of contract and recover damages for the inconvenience.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Substantial performance applies here because Bob acted in good faith, the defect is minor, and the essential purpose of the contract (a functional shed) was achieved. Ollie must pay the contract price minus the cost to remedy the defect. The UCC's perfect tender rule does not apply as this is a construction contract, not a sale of goods.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the elements of substantial performance: good faith, minor defects, and essential purpose achieved.

reveal.question: List three actions a non-breaching party can take when faced with an anticipatory breach.
reveal.answer:
The non-breaching party can:
1. Sue immediately for breach.
2. Wait for actual breach.
3. Treat the anticipatory breach as a material breach and discharge their own obligations under the contract.

rows.label: Applicability
values: Generally applies to service contracts (e.g., construction).
Applies to contracts for the sale of goods (UCC).

rows.label: Performance Standard
values: Requires good faith effort and minor defects are permissible if the essential purpose is achieved.
Requires exact conformity to the contract terms. Any deviation is a breach.

rows.label: Remedy
values: Non-breaching party must pay the contract price less the cost to remedy any defects.
Buyer can reject the goods, accept the goods, or accept part and reject part (subject to certain limitations).

title: Contracts: Third-Party Rights
description: Learn how contract rights can be transferred to third parties

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Contracts don't just affect the original parties! Assignment, delegation, and third-party beneficiaries extend contract rights and obligations to others. Understanding these concepts is essential for REG!

sections.title: Assignment of Rights
sections.content:
**Transfer of contract rights to third party:**

• **Assignor:** Original party transferring rights
• **Assignee:** Third party receiving rights
• **Obligor:** Party who must now perform to assignee

**No consideration required!**

**Generally assignable** unless contract prohibits or would materially change obligor's duty

sections.title: Rights that Cannot Be Assigned
content: Personal service contracts (unique skills)
Rights that would materially increase obligor's risk
Contracts expressly prohibiting assignment
Some wage assignments (limited by law)
Future rights not yet earned
Rights coupled with personal trust (attorney-client)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Assignment vs Delegation
sections.content:
**"Rights are ASSIGNED, Duties are DELEGATED"**

**Assignment:** "You get my right to receive $"
**Delegation:** "You do my job for me"

**Key difference:**
• Assignment transfers BENEFITS
• Delegation transfers BURDENS

sections.title: Delegation of Duties
sections.content:
**Transfer of contract duties to third party:**

• **Delegator:** Original party delegating duty
• **Delegatee:** Third party performing

**Generally delegable** unless:
• Personal skill/judgment required
• Contract prohibits delegation
• Would materially change performance

**Delegator remains liable unless novation!**

sections.title: Novation
sections.content:
**Substitution of parties:**

• New party completely replaces original party
• Original party released from liability
• Requires consent of ALL parties

**Novation ≠ Assignment**
• Assignment: Assignor still secondarily liable
• Novation: Original party completely released

sections.title: Intended vs Incidental Beneficiary
sections.content:
**Intended beneficiary:**
• Parties INTENDED to benefit this person
• Named or identifiable in contract
• Has ENFORCEABLE rights

**Incidental beneficiary:**
• Benefits by chance from contract
• NOT intended by parties
• NO enforceable rights

**Example:** City contracts for road repair → Businesses benefit = Incidental only

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: When Rights Vest
sections.content:
**Third-party beneficiary rights VEST when:**

• Beneficiary learns of contract and assents
• Beneficiary materially changes position
• Beneficiary sues to enforce

**Before vesting:** Original parties can modify/cancel
**After vesting:** Cannot modify without beneficiary's consent

**Timing matters!**

sections.title: Assignment Notice
sections.content:
**Notice to obligor:**

• Not required for valid assignment
• BUT strongly recommended!

**Without notice:**
• Obligor who pays assignor is discharged
• Assignee must sue assignor

**With notice:**
• Obligor must pay assignee
• Payment to assignor doesn't discharge

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Assignment transfers rights; Delegation transfers duties
Most rights assignable unless personal or prohibited
Delegator remains liable unless novation occurs
Novation requires ALL parties' consent
Intended beneficiaries can sue; Incidental cannot
Beneficiary rights vest once they rely or assent
Notice to obligor is important but not required for validity

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Ollie enters into a contract with Vance, where Ollie will paint Vance's house for $5,000. Ollie, needing immediate funds, assigns his right to receive payment to Wendy. Vance is notified of the assignment. Later, Vance pays Ollie the $5,000. Which of the following is correct?
options: Wendy can recover the $5,000 from Vance.
Wendy can only recover the $5,000 from Ollie.
Vance is not liable to Wendy because he paid the original party, Ollie.
The assignment is invalid because Ollie received consideration from Vance.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Once Vance was notified of the assignment, he was obligated to pay Wendy, the assignee. Paying Ollie does not discharge Vance's obligation to Wendy. Wendy can recover from Vance. Ollie is also liable to Wendy. Consideration is not required for the assignment.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider what happens when the obligor is notified of the assignment.

reveal.question: List three circumstances under which duties cannot be delegated.
reveal.answer: 1. Personal skill/judgment is required. 2. The contract prohibits delegation. 3. Delegation would materially change the performance.

rows.label: What is transferred?
values: Contract rights are transferred to a third party.
Contract duties are transferred to a third party.

rows.label: Original Party's Role
values: Assignor transfers rights.
Delegator transfers duties.

rows.label: Third Party's Role
values: Assignee receives rights.
Delegatee performs duties.

rows.label: Original Party's Liability
values: Assignor is typically not liable after assignment unless there are specific guarantees. Vance has to pay Wendy.
Delegator remains liable even after delegation. Ollie is still liable to Vance.

title: Business Structures: Overview
description: Compare the legal characteristics of different business entities

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Choosing the right business structure impacts liability, management, taxation, and more! Understanding the legal characteristics of each entity type helps CPAs advise clients and is essential for REG!

sections.title: Sole Proprietorship
sections.content:
**Simplest structure:**

• No formal creation required
• Owner has unlimited personal liability
• All income/losses on Schedule C
• No separate legal existence
• Terminates on owner's death

**Pros:** Simple, low cost, full control
**Cons:** Unlimited liability, limited capital raising

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Entity Characteristics
sections.content:
**"CALM"** for limited liability entities:

**C**orporation
**A**s an LLC
**L**imited Partnership (LPs only)
**M**embers/shareholders protected

**General Partners and Sole Proprietors = UNLIMITED liability**

sections.title: General Partnership
sections.content:
**Two or more persons carrying on business for profit:**

• Created by agreement (can be oral)
• Partners share profits/losses
• ALL partners have unlimited liability
• Joint and several liability for partnership debts
• Pass-through taxation

**Advantage:** Easy formation, flexibility
**Disadvantage:** Liability exposure

sections.title: Limited Partnership (LP)
sections.content:
**Has two classes of partners:**

**General Partners:**
• Manage business
• Unlimited liability

**Limited Partners:**
• Passive investors
• Limited liability (to investment)
• Cannot manage (or lose protection)

**Requires formal filing with state**

sections.title: Limited Liability Company (LLC)
sections.content:
**Best of both worlds:**

• Limited liability for ALL members
• Flexible management (member or manager-managed)
• Pass-through taxation (default)
• Can elect corporate taxation
• Operating agreement governs

**Most popular new business entity!**

**Requires state filing (Articles of Organization)**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: LP Participation
sections.content:
**Limited partner participating in management:**

**Old rule:** Lose limited liability protection
**Modern rule (RULPA):** More protected, but some risk remains

**Safe activities for LPs:**
• Voting on major matters
• Being employee/contractor
• Consulting on business

**Danger:** Acting as if you're a general partner

sections.title: Corporation Basics
sections.content:
**Separate legal entity:**

• Shareholders own; Directors manage
• Limited liability for shareholders
• Perpetual existence
• Formal requirements (minutes, meetings)

**C Corp:** Double taxation (corporate + dividend)
**S Corp:** Pass-through (but restrictions apply)

**Requires state filing (Articles of Incorporation)**

sections.title: Choosing an Entity
sections.content:
**Consider:**

• **Liability protection needed?** → LLC, Corp
• **Number of owners?** → S Corp limited to 100 shareholders
• **Tax treatment preference?** → Pass-through vs C Corp
• **Raising capital?** → Corp preferred by investors
• **Simplicity?** → Sole prop, Partnership
• **Formalities tolerable?** → Corp has most

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sole proprietorship: Simple but unlimited liability
General partnership: All partners have unlimited liability
Limited partnership: GPs unlimited, LPs limited (if passive)
LLC: Limited liability + tax flexibility
Corporation: Limited liability + formal requirements
S Corps and LLCs offer pass-through taxation
C Corps face double taxation

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: John is the sole proprietor of 'John's Widgets.' The business incurs a significant debt it cannot pay. Which of the following statements is correct regarding John's liability?
options: John has no personal liability for the debt because 'John's Widgets' is a separate legal entity.
John is only liable up to the amount of capital he initially invested in 'John's Widgets'.
John has unlimited personal liability for the debt, meaning his personal assets are at risk.
John's liability is limited to the business assets of 'John's Widgets'.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: As a sole proprietor, John has unlimited personal liability. This means his personal assets (e.g., house, savings) are at risk to satisfy the business debt. The sole proprietorship is not a separate legal entity.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the fundamental characteristic of a sole proprietorship regarding personal liability.

reveal.question: How are profits and losses from a general partnership taxed?
reveal.answer: General partnerships are subject to pass-through taxation. This means the partnership itself does not pay income tax. Instead, the profits and losses are passed through to the partners, who report their share of the income or loss on their individual income tax returns. Each partner's share is determined by the partnership agreement.

sections.title: Comparison: Sole Proprietorship vs. General Partnership

rows.label: Number of Owners
values: One
Two or More

rows.label: Liability
values: Unlimited personal liability
Unlimited personal liability (joint and several)

rows.label: Formal Creation
values: No formal creation required
Created by agreement (can be oral)

rows.label: Separate Legal Existence
values: No
No

title: Partnerships & LLCs: Legal Aspects
description: Understand partnership and LLC formation, operation, and dissolution

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Partnerships and LLCs are incredibly common business structures! Understanding the legal rules—especially partner rights, fiduciary duties, and dissolution—is critical for REG. Don't confuse legal rules (this lesson) with tax rules (covered later)!

sections.title: Partnership Formation
sections.content:
**Association of two or more persons:**

• Carrying on a business
• As co-owners
• For profit

**No formal agreement required!**

**Factors suggesting partnership:**
• Sharing profits (strongest factor)
• Sharing losses
• Sharing management
• Joint ownership of property

sections.title: Partner Rights
content: **Management** - Equal vote regardless of capital contribution
**Profits/Losses** - Share equally (unless agreed otherwise)
**Inspect books** - Full access to partnership records
**Accounting** - Right to formal accounting
**Partnership property** - Use for partnership purposes
**Indemnification** - Reimbursement for proper expenses

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Partner Duties
sections.content:
**Partners owe "LOC"** fiduciary duties:

**L**oyalty - Put partnership first
**O**bedience - Follow partnership agreement
**C**are - Act as prudent person

**Same as agent duties!** Partners are mutual agents.

sections.title: Partnership Liability
sections.content:
**Joint and several liability:**

• Each partner liable for ALL partnership debts
• Creditor can sue any partner for full amount
• Partner who pays can seek contribution from others

**Incoming partner:** Not liable for pre-existing debts (unless agreed)

**Outgoing partner:** Remains liable for debts incurred while partner (until creditor releases)

sections.title: Partnership Property vs Partner Property
sections.content:
**Partnership property:**
• Owned by partnership entity
• Used for partnership business
• Cannot be attached by personal creditors

**Partner's interest:**
• Right to share of profits/surplus
• Can be attached by personal creditors (charging order)
• Cannot force liquidation (generally)

sections.title: Dissolution vs Winding Up
sections.content:
**Dissolution = Change in partner relationship**
**Winding up = Process of terminating business**

**Causes of dissolution:**
• Express will of partner to withdraw
• Expulsion per agreement
• Bankruptcy of partner
• Death of partner (unless agreed otherwise)
• Court decree

**After dissolution:** Wind up or continue with buyout

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Partnership vs Partner Property
sections.content:
**Personal creditor of partner:**
• Can get "charging order" against partner's interest
• CANNOT seize partnership property
• CANNOT force dissolution (usually)

**Partnership creditor:**
• Can seize partnership property
• Partners personally liable if partnership assets insufficient

**Keep these creditor types straight!**

sections.title: LLC Formation
sections.content:
**Requires state filing:**

• **Articles of Organization** - Filed with state
• **Operating Agreement** - Governs internal affairs

**Members have:**
• Limited liability (like shareholders)
• Flexible management options
• Pass-through taxation (default)

**Operating agreement is key document!**

sections.title: LLC Management
sections.content:
**Two options:**

**Member-Managed:**
• All members participate in management
• Each member has authority to bind LLC
• Default rule

**Manager-Managed:**
• Designated managers run business
• Non-manager members are passive
• Members can hire outside managers

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Partnership can be oral; sharing profits is key factor
Partners share equally in profits/losses unless agreed
Joint and several liability for partnership debts
