Partner's personal creditors: Charging order only
Dissolution = Change in relationship; Winding up = Termination
LLC requires Articles of Organization filed with state
LLCs can be member-managed or manager-managed

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST important factor in determining whether a partnership exists?
options: Joint ownership of property used in the business.
Sharing gross revenues of a business.
Sharing profits of a business.
Having a written partnership agreement.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sharing profits is the strongest indicator of a partnership. While other factors can be suggestive, profit sharing carries the most weight. A written agreement is not required for a partnership to exist.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what the ultimate goal of a business is.

reveal.question: Describe the type of liability partners have for partnership debts.
reveal.answer: Partners have joint and several liability for partnership debts. This means that each partner is liable for the entire amount of the partnership's debts, and a creditor can pursue any one partner for the full amount.

knowledgeCheck.question: Unless otherwise agreed, what is the liability of an incoming partner for debts incurred by the partnership *before* their admission?
options: The incoming partner has unlimited personal liability for all pre-existing debts.
The incoming partner has no liability for any pre-existing debts.
The incoming partner's liability for pre-existing debts is limited to their capital contribution to the partnership.
The incoming partner is only liable for pre-existing debts if the other partners become insolvent.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: An incoming partner is not personally liable for the partnership's debts before they became a partner. However, their capital contribution to the partnership is at risk.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that a partner is only liable if they were a partner at the time the debt was incurred.

title: Corporations: Formation & Governance
description: Learn the legal requirements for forming and operating corporations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Corporations are the dominant form of large business organization! Understanding incorporation, the roles of shareholders/directors/officers, and corporate formalities is essential for REG and for advising business clients!

sections.title: Corporation Formation
sections.content:
**Steps to incorporate:**

1. **Choose state** - Delaware popular for flexibility
2. **File Articles of Incorporation** - With state
3. **Pay filing fees** - State fees vary
4. **Adopt Bylaws** - Internal operating rules
5. **Hold organizational meeting** - Elect directors, issue stock

**Corporation exists when articles filed (or effective date)**

sections.title: Articles of Incorporation
sections.content:
**Required contents (vary by state):**

• Corporate name (must include Corp., Inc., etc.)
• Registered agent and address
• Number of authorized shares
• Name(s) of incorporator(s)
• Corporate purpose (can be "any lawful purpose")

**Cannot be changed without shareholder approval**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Corporate Structure
sections.content:
**"SOD"** the hierarchy:

**S**hareholders - ELECT directors, approve major changes
**O**fficers - RUN daily operations
**D**irectors - SET policy, HIRE officers

**Shareholders own it → Directors govern it → Officers run it**

sections.title: Role of Shareholders
sections.content:
**Shareholders:**

• OWN the corporation (residual claim)
• ELECT directors (fundamental right)
• Vote on fundamental changes:
  - Mergers and acquisitions
  - Amendments to articles
  - Sale of substantially all assets
  - Dissolution

**Do NOT manage day-to-day operations!**

sections.title: Role of Directors
sections.content:
**Board of Directors:**

• Set policy and major decisions
• Hire and fire officers
• Declare dividends
• Approve major contracts
• Act through board meetings

**Owe fiduciary duties:** Duty of Care, Duty of Loyalty

**Business Judgment Rule:** Protects good-faith decisions

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Piercing the Corporate Veil
sections.content:
**Courts can hold shareholders personally liable if:**

• Corporation is "alter ego" of shareholder
• Commingling personal and corporate funds
• Inadequate capitalization
• Failure to observe corporate formalities
• Fraud or injustice

**Maintain separation!** Separate accounts, proper records, adequate capital

sections.title: Shareholder Meetings
sections.content:
**Annual meeting required:**
• Elect directors
• Other business as needed

**Special meetings:**
• Called by board or specified shareholders
• Only matters in notice can be addressed

**Quorum:** Majority of shares entitled to vote (usually)

**Proxy voting:** Shareholders can authorize others to vote

sections.title: Director Meetings
sections.content:
**Regular meetings:**
• No notice required if time set

**Special meetings:**
• Reasonable notice required

**Quorum:** Majority of directors

**Voting:** Majority of quorum approves

**Written consent:** Can act without meeting if unanimous written consent

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Corporation formed by filing Articles of Incorporation
Shareholders elect directors and approve major changes
Directors set policy and hire officers
Officers run daily operations
Directors owe fiduciary duties (Care, Loyalty)
Business judgment rule protects good-faith decisions
Piercing the veil if formalities not followed

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following actions is NOT typically required during the initial formation of a corporation?
options: Filing Articles of Incorporation with the state.
Adopting Bylaws to govern internal operations.
Electing officers to manage day-to-day operations.
Holding an organizational meeting to elect directors and issue stock.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Electing officers is generally done *after* the initial organizational meeting where directors are elected. The directors then appoint the officers.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the sequence of events in forming a corporation.

reveal.question: Name at least three required contents of the Articles of Incorporation.
reveal.answer: The Articles of Incorporation generally require the corporate name, the registered agent and address, the number of authorized shares, the name(s) of the incorporator(s), and the corporate purpose.

rows.label: Primary Role
values: Own the corporation and elect directors
Manage the corporation's business

rows.label: Day-to-Day Operations
values: Do NOT manage day-to-day operations
Responsible for day-to-day operations

rows.label: Voting Rights
values: Vote on fundamental changes and elect directors
Do NOT vote on fundamental changes in the same way shareholders do, but they make decisions regarding the business.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following corporate actions requires shareholder approval?
options: Hiring a new Chief Financial Officer (CFO).
Declaring a quarterly dividend.
Amending the Articles of Incorporation.
Entering into a routine supply contract.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Amendments to the Articles of Incorporation are fundamental changes that require shareholder approval. The other actions are typically within the purview of the board of directors or management.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about actions that fundamentally alter the corporation's structure or existence.

title: Secured Transactions: UCC Article 9
description: Master the rules for security interests in personal property

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Lenders want assurance they'll be repaid! Secured transactions under UCC Article 9 allow creditors to take a security interest in property. Understanding attachment, perfection, and priority is critical for REG!

sections.title: What is a Secured Transaction?
sections.content:
**Creditor takes interest in debtor's property:**

• **Secured party:** Creditor with security interest
• **Debtor:** Person who owes obligation
• **Collateral:** Property subject to security interest

**If debtor defaults:** Secured party can repossess collateral

**UCC Article 9** governs secured transactions in personal property

sections.title: Two-Step Process
sections.content:
**Step 1: ATTACHMENT** (creates security interest)
• Rights against debtor

**Step 2: PERFECTION** (gives priority)
• Rights against third parties

**Without attachment:** No security interest
**Without perfection:** Interest valid but may lose to others

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Attachment Requirements
sections.content:
**"VCR"** for attachment:

**V**alue - Creditor gave value
**C**ollateral - Debtor has rights in collateral
**R**ecord - Security agreement (authenticated) OR possession

**All three required! Record the VCR!**

sections.title: Security Agreement
sections.content:
**Must contain:**

• Debtor's authentication (signature)
• Description of collateral
• Intent to create security interest

**Description can be:**
• Specific ("2024 Toyota Camry VIN #...")
• By type ("all inventory")
• "All assets" NOT sufficient for some collateral

**After-acquired property clause:** Can cover future collateral

sections.title: Financing Statement (UCC-1)
sections.content:
**Filed with state (usually Secretary of State):**

**Must contain:**
• Debtor's name (EXACT!)
• Secured party's name
• Collateral description

**Duration:** 5 years (can file continuation)

**Where to file:** State where debtor is located (organization's state of incorporation)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: PMSI (Purchase Money Security Interest)
sections.content:
**PMSI = Creditor financed the collateral purchase**

**Super-priority over prior perfected interests IF:**

**Inventory:** PMSI perfected BEFORE delivery AND notice sent to prior secured parties

**Non-inventory:** PMSI perfected within 20 days of delivery

**Consumer goods:** PMSI automatically perfected (no filing needed)!

sections.title: Priority Rules
sections.content:
**General priority (first in time):**

1. **Perfected > Unperfected** (always)
2. **Between perfected:** First to file or perfect
3. **Between unperfected:** First to attach

**Exceptions:**
• PMSI has super-priority
• Buyers in ordinary course take free of security interests

sections.title: Default and Remedies
sections.content:
**Upon default, secured party can:**

• Take possession (peacefully or by court)
• Dispose of collateral (sale, lease)
• Keep collateral in satisfaction (strict foreclosure)

**Disposition must be commercially reasonable**

**Proceeds:** Apply to debt; surplus to debtor; deficiency owed by debtor

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Attachment requires: Value, Collateral rights, Record (VCR)
Perfection gives priority over third parties
Most perfection by filing financing statement (UCC-1)
Debtor's name must be EXACT on financing statement
PMSI gets super-priority (special timing rules)
Consumer goods PMSI: Automatic perfection
Perfected beats unperfected; First to file/perfect wins

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is correct regarding the difference between attachment and perfection in secured transactions under UCC Article 9?
options: Attachment grants the secured party rights against third parties, while perfection grants rights against the debtor.
Attachment requires filing a financing statement, while perfection only requires a security agreement.
Attachment establishes the security interest between the debtor and secured party, while perfection gives the secured party priority over other creditors.
Attachment is optional, while perfection is always required for a valid security interest.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Attachment creates the security interest between the debtor and secured party. Perfection provides the secured party with priority over other creditors who may have a claim to the same collateral. Without attachment, there is no security interest at all.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider who each step is protecting the secured party against.

reveal.question: What are the three essential requirements for a valid security agreement under UCC Article 9?
reveal.answer: 1. Debtor's authentication (signature). 2. Description of the collateral. 3. Intent to create a security interest.

knowledgeCheck.question: Debtor, David, grants Secured Party, Bank, a security interest. Which of the following descriptions of collateral in the security agreement would be considered insufficient under UCC Article 9, thereby potentially invalidating the security interest in the described property?
options: "All equipment located at 123 Main Street, Anytown, USA"
"All inventory"
"2023 Ford F-150, VIN: 12345ABCDEFG67890"
"All assets"
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under UCC Article 9, a description of 'all assets' is generally NOT sufficient for some types of collateral. A more specific description is required to provide adequate notice to third parties. While some jurisdictions may allow 'all assets' for certain types of collateral, it is generally considered insufficient and therefore the best answer here. The other options provide a sufficiently specific description.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what provides the MOST detail and clarity to other potential creditors.

title: Bankruptcy: Chapters 7, 11, 13
description: Understand the major types of bankruptcy proceedings

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Bankruptcy affects creditors, debtors, and businesses alike! Understanding the differences between liquidation (Ch. 7), reorganization (Ch. 11), and individual debt adjustment (Ch. 13) is essential for advising clients and passing REG!

sections.title: Chapter 7: Liquidation
sections.content:
**"Fresh start" through liquidation:**

• Trustee collects and sells non-exempt assets
• Proceeds distributed to creditors by priority
• Remaining debts discharged (with exceptions)

**Means test:** Individuals must qualify (income below median)

**Not available:** If income above median and can pay some debts

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Bankruptcy Chapters
sections.content:
**"7-11-13"** like convenience stores:

**7** = LIQUIDATE (sell everything, quick)
**11** = REORGANIZE (keep business running)
**13** = REPAY over time (wage earner plan)

**7 = Sell | 11 = Save business | 13 = Salary pays**

sections.title: Voluntary vs Involuntary
sections.content:
**Voluntary:** Debtor files petition

**Involuntary (creditors force):**
• Only Chapters 7 and 11
• If 12+ creditors: Need 3 petitioning creditors
• If fewer than 12: One creditor sufficient
• Total claims must be at least ~$18,600
• Debtor not paying debts as they become due

**Cannot file involuntary against:** Farmers, nonprofits

sections.title: Automatic Stay
sections.content:
**Immediate upon filing:**

• ALL collection efforts STOP
• No lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures
• No phone calls demanding payment
• Secured creditors can seek relief from stay

**Powerful protection for debtor!**

**Violation = Contempt of court**

sections.title: Priority of Claims
content: **1. Secured creditors** (from collateral proceeds)
**2. Domestic support obligations** (alimony, child support)
**3. Administrative expenses** (trustee fees, attorney fees)
**4. Gap claims** (involuntary - between filing and order)
**5. Employee wages** (up to ~$15,150, within 180 days)
**6. Employee benefit plan contributions**
**7. Consumer deposits** (up to ~$3,350)
**8. Tax claims** (certain taxes)
**9. General unsecured creditors** (pro rata)

sections.title: Non-Dischargeable Debts
sections.content:
**Cannot be discharged (survive bankruptcy):**

• Student loans (unless undue hardship)
• Alimony and child support
• Most taxes (within 3 years)
• Debts from fraud or false statements
• Debts not listed in petition
• Fines and penalties to government
• Personal injury from DUI

**These must still be paid!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Student Loans
sections.content:
**Student loans are generally NOT dischargeable!**

**Exception:** Prove "undue hardship" (very difficult standard)

**Common misconception:** Filing bankruptcy eliminates student loans → FALSE

**They survive both Chapter 7 AND Chapter 13!**

sections.title: Chapter 11: Reorganization
sections.content:
**Business continues operating:**

• Debtor usually remains in possession ("DIP")
• Files reorganization plan
• Creditors vote on plan
• Court confirms plan
• Business pays debts over time

**Allows business to survive and pay creditors more than liquidation**

sections.title: Chapter 13: Wage Earner Plan
sections.content:
**Individual debt adjustment:**

• Regular income required
• Keep property while repaying
• 3-5 year repayment plan
• Debt limits apply (~$465,000 unsecured)

**Advantages over Ch. 7:**
• Keep property
• More debts may be discharged
• Catch up on mortgage/car payments

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Chapter 7: Liquidation, fresh start, means test required
Chapter 11: Business reorganization, debtor stays in control
Chapter 13: Wage earner repayment plan, 3-5 years
Automatic stay stops ALL collection immediately
Priority: Secured → Support → Admin → Wages → Taxes → Unsecured
Student loans, support, and recent taxes NOT dischargeable
Involuntary: Only Ch. 7 and 11, requires creditor petition

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: An individual debtor with income above the state median files for Chapter 7 bankruptcy. After deducting allowable expenses, it is determined that the debtor could pay a significant portion of their unsecured debts over a five-year period. Which of the following is most likely to occur?
options: The debtor will automatically be denied Chapter 7 bankruptcy.
The court will convert the case to Chapter 13 bankruptcy if the debtor consents.
The court will dismiss the Chapter 7 case or convert it to Chapter 13 with the debtor's consent, based on the means test.
The debtor will be required to reaffirm their debts and establish a repayment plan outside of bankruptcy.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The means test is used to determine if a debtor with above-median income can qualify for Chapter 7. If the debtor has sufficient disposable income to repay a significant portion of their debts, the court may dismiss the Chapter 7 case or convert it to Chapter 13, provided the debtor agrees to the conversion.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the purpose of the means test in Chapter 7 bankruptcy.

reveal.question: What is the minimum number of creditors required to file an involuntary Chapter 7 bankruptcy petition if the debtor has 12 or more creditors?
reveal.answer: Three creditors are required to file an involuntary Chapter 7 bankruptcy petition if the debtor has 12 or more creditors. These creditors must collectively hold at least $18,600 in unsecured claims as of 2023.

rows.label: Initiation
values: Debtor files petition
Creditors file petition

rows.label: Applicable Chapters
values: Chapter 7, 11, 13
Chapter 7 and 11 only

rows.label: Debtor Solvency
values: Debtor's solvency is not a barrier
Debtor must generally not be paying debts as they become due

rows.label: Creditor Requirements
values: None
Specific requirements for number of creditors and amount of claims

title: Secured Transactions: UCC Article 9
description: Master secured transactions including attachment, perfection, and priority

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Secured transactions govern how creditors protect their interests in collateral. Understanding attachment, perfection, and priority is essential for advising business clients and is heavily tested on REG!

sections.title: What is a Security Interest?
sections.content:
**Security interest = Interest in personal property that secures payment**

**Key terms:**
• **Debtor** - Person who owes the debt
• **Secured party** - Creditor with security interest
• **Collateral** - Property securing the debt
• **Security agreement** - Contract creating security interest

sections.title: Attachment: Creating Security Interest
sections.content:
**Three requirements for attachment:**

1. **Value given** - Creditor gives something (loan, credit)
2. **Debtor has rights** - In the collateral
3. **Security agreement** - Authenticated description of collateral

**When all three occur = Security interest ATTACHES**

**Attachment makes it enforceable against debtor**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Attachment - VRS
sections.content:
**"VRS"** for Attachment:

**V**alue given by secured party
**R**ights in collateral (debtor has)
**S**ecurity agreement (authenticated)

**"Very Risky Securities need all three!"**

sections.title: Perfection: Protecting Against Third Parties
sections.content:
**Perfection = Notice to the world of security interest**

**Methods of perfection:**
• **Filing** - UCC-1 financing statement (most common)
• **Possession** - Creditor takes collateral
• **Control** - Bank accounts, investment securities
• **Automatic** - PMSIs in consumer goods

**Perfection protects against other creditors!**

sections.title: Purchase Money Security Interest (PMSI)
sections.content:
**PMSI = Security interest in goods securing their purchase price**

**Example:** Bank loans money to buy equipment, takes security interest in that equipment

**Special priority rules:**
• PMSI in inventory - Must perfect BEFORE delivery
• PMSI in other goods - 20 days after delivery to perfect

**PMSIs get SUPER-PRIORITY over earlier-filed interests!**

sections.title: Priority Rules
rows: First to file or perfect wins
Buyer takes free of security interest

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Buyer in Ordinary Course
sections.content:
**A buyer in ordinary course of business takes FREE of security interest!**

**Example:**
• Bank has security interest in store's inventory
• Customer buys item from store (ordinary course)
• Customer owns item FREE of bank's security interest

**Buyer must not know interest is unauthorized**

sections.title: Default and Remedies
sections.content:
**Upon default, secured party may:**

• Take possession of collateral (self-help if no breach of peace)
• Sell collateral (commercially reasonable manner)
• Accept collateral in satisfaction (strict foreclosure)
• Sue on the debt

**Surplus goes to debtor; deficiency owed by debtor**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Attachment: Value + Rights + Security Agreement
Perfection: Filing, possession, control, or automatic
File UCC-1 with Secretary of State (5-year duration)
First to file or perfect generally wins priority
PMSI has super-priority if timely perfected
Buyer in ordinary course takes free of security interest
Upon default: Repossess, sell, apply proceeds

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT required for a security interest to attach?
options: The secured party gives value.
The debtor has rights in the collateral.
A security agreement exists that describes the collateral.
The secured party perfects the security interest.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Perfection is not required for attachment. Attachment establishes the security interest between the debtor and the secured party. Perfection protects the secured party's interest against third parties.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what creates the initial agreement vs. what protects it from others.

reveal.question: Define 'Perfection' in the context of secured transactions.
reveal.answer: Perfection is the process by which a secured party provides notice to the world of their security interest in the collateral, thereby protecting their interest against other creditors who may also have a claim against the same collateral. It is generally achieved through filing a financing statement, taking possession of the collateral, or control.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Creates security interest enforceable against the debtor.
Protects the secured party's interest against third parties (other creditors).

rows.label: Requirements
values: Value given, debtor has rights in collateral, security agreement.
Attachment AND taking steps to notify third parties (e.g., filing, possession, control).

rows.label: Timing
values: Occurs before or simultaneously with perfection.
Occurs after attachment.

title: Business Entity Formation: Sole Proprietorships
description: Understand the simplest form of business organization

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The sole proprietorship is the most common and simplest business form! Understanding its advantages and limitations helps advise clients choosing an entity structure. REG tests entity selection extensively!

sections.title: What is a Sole Proprietorship?
sections.content:
**One person owns and operates the business**

• No separate legal entity
• Owner IS the business
• No formal filing required to start
• May need licenses/permits

**Default structure when individual starts business alone**

sections.title: Formation Requirements
sections.content:
**Simple formation:**

• Just start doing business!
• May need:
  - Business license (local)
  - DBA/Fictitious name filing
  - Industry-specific permits
  - EIN (if employees)

**No state filing required for the entity itself**

sections.title: Advantages
content: **Easy to form** - No paperwork or legal fees
**Complete control** - Owner makes all decisions
**Simple taxation** - Report on Schedule C
**No double taxation** - Income flows to personal return
**Easy to dissolve** - Just stop doing business
**Minimal compliance** - No annual reports or meetings

sections.title: Disadvantages
content: **Unlimited personal liability** - Owner liable for ALL business debts
**Self-employment tax** - On all net earnings (15.3%)
**Limited capital raising** - Cannot sell ownership interests
**Limited life** - Ends when owner dies/stops
**No employee benefits** - Cannot be own employee

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Sole Prop Characteristics
sections.content:
**"SIMPLE but RISKY"**

**SIMPLE:**
• Single owner
• Individual control
• Minimal paperwork
• Personal return (Schedule C)
• Low cost
• Easy start/stop

**RISKY = Unlimited personal liability!**

sections.title: Tax Treatment
sections.content:
**Schedule C (Form 1040):**

• Report income and expenses
• Net profit = Self-employment income
• Subject to:
  - Income tax (regular rates)
  - Self-employment tax (15.3%)
  - Deduct 50% of SE tax above the line

**Quarterly estimated payments typically required**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Liability
sections.content:
**Business debts = Personal debts!**

**Example:** Sole proprietor's business is sued for $500,000

**At risk:**
• Business assets
• Personal home
• Personal bank accounts
• Personal investments
• ALL personal assets!

**No liability protection whatsoever**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Simplest business form - no state filing required
Owner has complete control but unlimited liability
Income reported on Schedule C (Form 1040)
Subject to self-employment tax (15.3%)
Default structure for individual doing business alone
Cannot raise capital by selling ownership
Business ends when owner dies or stops operating

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following actions is ALWAYS required to legally establish a sole proprietorship?
options: Filing articles of organization with the state.
Obtaining an Employer Identification Number (EIN) from the IRS.
Obtaining a business license from the local government.
None of the above; no formal filing is required at the state level to establish a sole proprietorship.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sole proprietorships are the simplest form of business to establish. Unlike corporations or LLCs, they do not require formal filing with the state. However, depending on the nature of the business and its location, licenses, permits, or a DBA may be necessary.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the defining characteristic of a sole proprietorship - it's extension of the owner.

reveal.question: Is a sole proprietorship considered a separate legal entity from its owner?
reveal.answer: No, a sole proprietorship is NOT a separate legal entity from its owner. The owner and the business are considered one and the same for legal and tax purposes.

sections.title: Knowledge Check: When is an EIN Required?

knowledgeCheck.question: Under which of the following circumstances would a sole proprietor MOST likely be required to obtain an Employer Identification Number (EIN) from the IRS?
options: When the sole proprietor's business generates more than $10,000 in annual revenue.
When the sole proprietor intends to incorporate the business in the future.
When the sole proprietor hires employees.
When the sole proprietor's business operates under a name different from the owner's legal name (DBA).
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A sole proprietor is generally required to obtain an EIN if they hire employees. While a DBA (doing business as) might be used, it doesn't automatically trigger the need for an EIN unless employees are hired. Revenue levels and future incorporation plans do not necessitate an EIN on their own.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about why the IRS assigns EINs.

title: Business Entity Formation: LLCs
description: Master Limited Liability Company formation and characteristics

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: LLCs combine the best features of corporations and partnerships! They're the most popular choice for new businesses. Understanding LLC formation, liability protection, and tax flexibility is essential for REG!

sections.title: What is an LLC?
sections.content:
**Limited Liability Company:**

• Separate legal entity from owners
• Owners called "members"
• Combines liability protection with tax flexibility
• State law entity (not federal)

**Best of both worlds: Corp liability + Partnership taxation**

sections.title: Formation Requirements
sections.content:
**To form an LLC:**

1. **File Articles of Organization** with state
2. **Choose a name** (must include "LLC" or similar)
3. **Designate registered agent**
4. **Pay filing fees**

**Operating Agreement** (highly recommended but not always required):
• Governs internal affairs
• Member rights and duties
• Profit/loss allocation
• Management structure

sections.title: Liability Protection
sections.content:
**Members have LIMITED LIABILITY:**

• Generally not personally liable for LLC debts
• At risk only for capital contribution
• Personal assets protected

**Exceptions (piercing the veil):**
• Personal guarantees
• Commingling funds
• Undercapitalization
• Failure to observe formalities

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: LLC Benefits
sections.content:
**"FLIP"** for LLC advantages:

**F**lexibility in management
**L**imited liability protection
**I**ncome flows through (no double tax)
**P**ass-through tax treatment (default)

**FLIP to an LLC for protection + flexibility!**

sections.title: Tax Classification (Check-the-Box)
sections.content:
**LLCs choose their tax treatment:**

**Single-member LLC:**
• Default: Disregarded entity (Schedule C)
• Can elect: S Corp or C Corp

**Multi-member LLC:**
• Default: Partnership (Form 1065)
• Can elect: S Corp or C Corp

**File Form 8832 to change classification**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Self-Employment Tax
sections.content:
**LLC members may owe SE tax!**

**Active members:** Usually subject to SE tax on distributive share

**S Corp election advantage:**
• Pay reasonable salary (FICA on wages only)
• Take distributions (no SE tax)

**LLC taxed as partnership = More SE tax exposure**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: LLC provides liability protection + tax flexibility
Form by filing Articles of Organization with state
Operating Agreement governs internal affairs
Default tax: Disregarded (single) or Partnership (multi)
Can elect S Corp or C Corp taxation
Member-managed (default) or manager-managed
Limited liability but watch for piercing the veil

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following actions is NOT required to form a Limited Liability Company (LLC)?
options: Filing Articles of Organization with the state.
Adopting bylaws that govern the internal operations of the LLC.
Choosing a name that includes 'LLC' or a similar designation.
Designating a registered agent.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While an operating agreement is highly recommended to govern internal affairs, bylaws are typically associated with corporations, not LLCs. Filing Articles of Organization, choosing a name with the proper designation, and designating a registered agent are all requirements for forming an LLC.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which documents are associated with corporations versus LLCs.

reveal.question: What is the primary purpose of an LLC's Operating Agreement?
reveal.answer: The Operating Agreement governs the internal affairs of the LLC and outlines the rights and duties of its members. It is a crucial document that provides clarity and structure to the LLC's operations, although it is not always legally required.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios would MOST likely result in a member of an LLC being held personally liable for the LLC's debts?
options: The LLC is unable to pay its debts due to poor market conditions.
A member personally guaranteed a bank loan taken out by the LLC.
The LLC experiences a significant loss due to a lawsuit.
A member contributed capital to the LLC, which was subsequently used to pay business expenses.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Members of an LLC generally have limited liability and are not personally responsible for the LLC's debts. However, this protection can be pierced in certain situations. A personal guarantee creates a direct obligation for the member to repay the loan if the LLC defaults.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which actions create a direct personal obligation separate from the LLC.

title: Partnership Formation and General Partners
description: Understand partnership formation, rights, and liabilities

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Partnerships are common business structures and heavily tested on REG! Understanding formation, partner duties, and especially the JOINT AND SEVERAL LIABILITY of general partners is critical for advising clients!

sections.title: What is a Partnership?
sections.content:
**Association of two or more persons to carry on a business for profit**

• No formal filing required (general partnership)
• Can be formed by conduct alone
• Partnership agreement recommended
• Each partner is an agent of the partnership

sections.title: Partnership Formation
sections.content:
**No formalities required for GP:**

• Oral or written agreement
• Intent to share profits
• Co-ownership of business
• Can form accidentally!

**Partnership agreement should address:**
• Capital contributions
• Profit/loss sharing
• Management rights
• Dissolution procedures

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Formation Elements
sections.content:
**"PIC"** creates a Partnership:

**P**rofit sharing intent
**I**ntent to do business together
**C**o-ownership of business

**Sharing profits creates PRESUMPTION of partnership!**

sections.title: Partner Rights
content: **Equal management rights** (unless agreed otherwise)
**Equal share of profits** (default rule)
**Indemnification** for proper partnership expenses
**Access to books** and records
**Compensation** only for winding up (default)
**New partners** require unanimous consent

sections.title: Partner Duties
sections.content:
**Fiduciary duties to partnership:**

• **Duty of Loyalty** - No competing, no self-dealing
• **Duty of Care** - Refrain from gross negligence
• **Duty of Good Faith** - Fair dealing

**Cannot eliminate duty of loyalty or good faith by agreement**

sections.title: Partner Liability
sections.content:
**JOINT AND SEVERAL LIABILITY:**

• Each general partner liable for ALL partnership debts
• Creditor can sue any one partner for entire debt
• Partners can seek contribution from others
• Personal assets at risk!

**Incoming partner:** Liable only for new debts (not prior)
**Outgoing partner:** Remains liable unless released

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Joint and Several
sections.content:
**Partnership has $1M debt, 4 partners:**

**Can creditor sue one partner for $1M?** YES!

• Each partner liable for entire amount
• That partner seeks contribution from others
• If others can't pay, one partner stuck with full debt

**This is the BIGGEST RISK of general partnerships!**

sections.title: Partnership Property
sections.content:
**Property contributed becomes partnership property:**

• Titled in partnership name
• Used for partnership purposes
• Purchased with partnership funds

**Partners have no individual rights in specific property**

**Interest in partnership is personal property**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Partnership: 2+ persons carrying on business for profit
No formal filing required for general partnership
Sharing profits creates presumption of partnership
General partners have joint and several liability
Fiduciary duties: Loyalty, care, good faith
Equal management and profit sharing (default)
Incoming partner: Not liable for pre-existing debts

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a required element for the formation of a general partnership?
options: A written partnership agreement.
Intent to share profits.
Co-ownership of the business.
Association of two or more persons to carry on a business for profit.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A written partnership agreement is highly recommended, but not legally required for the formation of a general partnership. A partnership can be formed orally or even implied through conduct. The other options are essential elements.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the minimum necessary requirements vs. best practices.

reveal.question: Define a general partnership in one sentence.
reveal.answer: A general partnership is an association of two or more persons to carry on a business for profit, without requiring formal filings.

rows.label: Formation Requirement
values: Not required for formation; can be formed by conduct alone.
None: A written agreement is never legally required to form a general partnership.

rows.label: Content
values: Should address capital contributions, profit/loss sharing, management rights, dissolution procedures, etc.
N/A

rows.label: Legal Standing
values: Provides clarity and legal enforceability regarding partner rights and responsibilities.
Absence can lead to disputes and application of default partnership laws which may not be ideal.

title: Corporation Formation and Characteristics
description: Master corporate formation, governance, and shareholder rights

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Corporations are the most formal business structure with the strongest liability protection! Understanding formation, governance, and shareholder rights is essential for REG and advising business clients!

sections.title: What is a Corporation?
sections.content:
**Separate legal entity created under state law**

• Distinct from its owners (shareholders)
• Can own property, sue, be sued
• Perpetual existence (survives owners)
• Centralized management (board of directors)

**Limited liability for shareholders**

sections.title: Formation: Articles of Incorporation
sections.content:
**File Articles with Secretary of State:**

**Required provisions:**
• Corporate name (must include "Inc." or similar)
• Number of authorized shares
• Registered agent and office
• Incorporator name(s)

**Optional provisions:**
• Purpose (any lawful purpose)
• Par value of shares
• Director names

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Corporate Hierarchy
sections.content:
**"SBO"** - Top to Bottom:

**S**hareholders (owners - vote)
**B**oard (directors - oversee)
**O**fficers (managers - operate)

**"Shareholders elect Board who hire Officers"**

sections.title: Shareholder Rights
content: **Vote** for directors and major changes
**Receive dividends** if declared by board
**Inspect** books and records (proper purpose)
**Sue derivatively** on behalf of corporation
**Preemptive rights** to maintain ownership % (if granted)
**Share in liquidation** proceeds (after creditors)

sections.title: Board of Directors Duties
sections.content:
**Fiduciary duties:**

• **Duty of Care** - Informed decisions, reasonable inquiry
• **Duty of Loyalty** - No self-dealing, corporate opportunity
• **Business Judgment Rule** - Protects good faith decisions

**Board sets policy; does NOT manage day-to-day**

sections.title: Piercing the Corporate Veil
sections.content:
**When shareholders lose liability protection:**

• **Commingling** - Personal and corporate funds mixed
• **Undercapitalization** - Inadequate funding
• **Alter ego** - No separation between owner and corp
• **Fraud** - Corp used to perpetrate fraud
• **Formality failures** - No meetings, minutes, records

**Courts can hold shareholders personally liable!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Formalities Matter
sections.content:
**To maintain liability protection:**

✓ Hold annual shareholder meetings
✓ Maintain minutes and records
✓ Keep separate bank accounts
✓ Adequately capitalize the business
✓ Use corporate name on contracts

**Skip formalities = Risk personal liability!**

sections.title: De Jure vs De Facto Corporation
sections.content:
**De Jure Corporation:**
• Properly formed under state law
• Full liability protection

**De Facto Corporation:**
• Good faith attempt to incorporate
• Colorable compliance with law
• Business conducted as corporation
• Limited protection from most creditors

**Corporation by Estoppel:** One who deals with entity as corporation cannot later deny it

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Corporation is separate legal entity from owners
Form by filing Articles of Incorporation with state
Shareholders elect board; board hires officers
Directors owe fiduciary duties (care, loyalty)
Business judgment rule protects good faith decisions
Piercing veil: Commingling, undercapitalization, fraud
Maintain formalities to preserve liability protection

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of a corporation?
options: Limited liability for shareholders
Perpetual existence
Direct management by shareholders
Separate legal entity
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Corporations have centralized management through a board of directors, not direct management by shareholders. Shareholders elect the board, who then manage the corporation.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about who makes the day-to-day decisions in a large corporation.

reveal.question: What is the document that must be filed with the Secretary of State to form a corporation?
reveal.answer: The Articles of Incorporation. Filing these articles creates the corporation as a legal entity.

knowledgeCheck.question: When forming a corporation, which of the following provisions MUST be included in the Articles of Incorporation?
options: The corporation's intended business purpose
The par value of the corporation's shares
The names of all initial directors
The number of authorized shares
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The number of authorized shares is a required provision. The corporation's purpose, par value, and director names are optional.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the core elements necessary to define the corporation's structure and legal standing.

rows.label: Corporate Name
values: Must include 'Inc.' or similar
Not applicable

rows.label: Number of Shares
values: Number of authorized shares
Not applicable

rows.label: Purpose
values: Not applicable
Any lawful purpose

rows.label: Par Value
values: Not applicable
Par value of shares

title: Gross Income: Inclusions
description: Master what items must be included in gross income

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Gross income is the starting point for all tax calculations! Section 61 defines it broadly—"all income from whatever source derived." Understanding what's included (and what's not) is fundamental to REG!

sections.title: Section 61: All-Inclusive Definition
sections.content:
**Gross income includes ALL income from whatever source:**

• Cash received
• Property received (FMV)
• Services received (FMV)
• Debt cancellation
• Illegal income

**"If it's income, it's probably taxable unless specifically excluded!"**

sections.title: Common Income Inclusions
rows: Offset by losses to extent of winnings

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Income Sources
sections.content:
**"WILD BIRD"** includes income from:

**W**ages
**I**nterest
**L**ottery/gambling
**D**ividends

**B**usiness
**I**llegal activities
**R**ents/royalties
**D**ebt cancellation

**All taxable unless specifically excluded!**

sections.title: Constructive Receipt
sections.content:
**Income is received when:**

• Actually received, OR
• **Constructively received** - Available without substantial limitation

**Examples:**
• Check mailed in December = December income (even if deposited in January)
• Interest credited to account = Income when credited
• Bonus earned but deferred = Not constructively received if legitimate deferral

**Cash basis taxpayers recognize when received!**

sections.title: Compensation Issues
sections.content:
**Taxable compensation includes:**

• Cash wages/salary
• Bonuses
• Stock options (taxed on exercise or sale)
• Fringe benefits (unless excluded)
• Severance pay
• Back pay awards

**Non-cash:** Include at FMV on date received

sections.title: Alimony (Divorce Timing Matters!)
sections.content:
**Divorce/separation agreements:**

**Before 2019:**
• Alimony = Taxable to recipient, deductible by payer

**After 2018 (TCJA):**
• Alimony = NOT taxable, NOT deductible

**Child support:** Never taxable or deductible (regardless of date)

**Property settlements:** Generally no tax consequence

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Pre-2019 vs Post-2018 Alimony
sections.content:
**The exam WILL test this!**

**Agreement date matters:**

• Executed BEFORE 2019 → Old rules (taxable/deductible)
• Executed AFTER 2018 → New rules (nothing)

**Modified agreements:** Old rules continue unless modification expressly adopts new rules

**Read the dates carefully!**

sections.title: Debt Cancellation
sections.content:
**Cancellation of Debt (COD) income:**

• Generally taxable (Form 1099-C)
• Treated as if debtor received cash

**Exclusions (not taxable):**
• Bankruptcy discharge
• Insolvency (to extent insolvent)
• Qualified farm debt
• Qualified real property business debt
• Qualified principal residence debt (through 2025)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Gross income includes ALL income unless specifically excluded
Constructive receipt: Available = Received (cash basis)
Non-cash income taxed at FMV
Alimony: Pre-2019 = taxable; Post-2018 = not taxable
Child support: NEVER taxable or deductible
Debt cancellation is income (with exclusions)
Illegal income is still taxable!

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: In December Year 1, Sarah completed a consulting project and billed her client $5,000. The client mailed a check to Sarah on December 28, Year 1, which Sarah received on January 2, Year 2. Also, in Year 1, Sarah won a lottery prize of $1,000, but she instructed the lottery commission to mail the check to her on January 5, Year 2. What amount of income should Sarah include in her gross income for Year 1?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The $5,000 consulting fee is included in Year 1 gross income because Sarah constructively received the income when the check was mailed on December 28, Year 1. The $1,000 lottery prize is included in Year 2 because Sarah intentionally deferred receipt, meaning she did not have constructive receipt in Year 1.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the concept of constructive receipt. When is the income available to the taxpayer without substantial limitations?

sections.title: Test Your Understanding of Constructive Receipt

reveal.question: Define 'constructive receipt' for tax purposes.
reveal.answer: Constructive receipt occurs when income is credited to a taxpayer's account, set apart for them, or otherwise made available so that they may draw upon it at any time, or so that they could have drawn upon it during the taxable year if notice of intention to withdraw had been given. However, income is not constructively received if the taxpayer's control of its receipt is subject to substantial limitations or restrictions.

knowledgeCheck.question: John illegally sells narcotics and receives $50,000 in cash during the year. He uses $20,000 of this income to purchase a car and spends the rest on living expenses. What amount, if any, must John include in his gross income for the year?
options: $0, because it is illegal income.
$30,000, the amount remaining after purchasing the car.
$50,000, the full amount of illegal income.
The amount of net profit after subtracting ordinary and necessary business expenses.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Illegal income is included in gross income. The source of the income is irrelevant; if it's income, it's taxable unless specifically excluded. Therefore, John must include the full $50,000 in his gross income.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the all-inclusive definition of gross income: 'all income from whatever source.'

title: Gross Income: Exclusions
description: Learn what items can be excluded from gross income

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Not everything is taxable! The Code provides specific exclusions from gross income. Knowing these exclusions can save clients significant taxes—and they're heavily tested on REG!

sections.title: Major Exclusions from Gross Income
content: **Gifts and inheritances** (Section 102)
**Life insurance proceeds** (Section 101)
**Municipal bond interest** (Section 103)
**Qualified scholarships** (Section 117)
**Employer-provided health insurance**
**Workers' compensation** (Section 104)
**Certain fringe benefits** (Section 132)
**Foreign earned income** (Section 911)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Exclusions
sections.content:
**"GLIMS"** are excluded:

**G**ifts and inheritances
**L**ife insurance death benefits
**I**njury compensation (workers' comp, damages)
**M**unicipal bond interest
**S**cholarships (qualified)

**These are TAX-FREE!**

sections.title: Gifts vs Compensation
sections.content:
**Gift = Transfer from detached generosity**

**NOT a gift (taxable):**
• Tips (payment for services)
• Employer "gifts" to employees
• Payments for past services

**True gift:** Motivated by affection, respect, charity

**Inheritance:** Always excluded (but income FROM inherited property is taxable)

sections.title: Life Insurance Proceeds
sections.content:
**Death benefits generally excluded:**

• Lump sum to beneficiary = Tax-free
• Installments = Principal excluded, interest taxable

**Exceptions (taxable):**
• Transfer for valuable consideration
• Employer-owned life insurance (EOLI) unless exceptions met

**Accelerated death benefits:** Excluded if terminally/chronically ill

sections.title: Qualified Scholarships
sections.content:
**Excluded if used for:**

• Tuition and fees
• Books and supplies (required)

**Taxable (NOT excluded):**
• Room and board
• Travel
• Equipment not required

**Must be degree candidate at educational institution**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Group-Term Life Insurance
sections.content:
**Only first $50,000 of coverage excluded!**

**Over $50,000:**
• "Cost" of excess coverage is taxable
• Use IRS Table I to determine includible amount
• Based on employee's age

**Example:** $100,000 policy
• $50,000 excluded
• Imputed income on $50,000 excess coverage

sections.title: Damages and Settlements
sections.content:
**Personal physical injury/sickness:**
• Compensatory damages = Excluded
• Medical expense reimbursement = Excluded

**NOT excluded (taxable):**
• Punitive damages (always taxable)
• Emotional distress damages (unless physical origin)
• Lost wages (unless part of physical injury)
• Interest on judgment

**Physical injury required for exclusion!**

sections.title: Foreign Earned Income Exclusion
sections.content:
**Section 911 exclusion:**

**2024:** Up to $126,500 excluded

**Requirements:**
• Tax home in foreign country
• Meet physical presence test (330 days in 12-month period), OR
• Bona fide residence test

**Plus:** Housing exclusion/deduction available

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Gifts and inheritances: Excluded from recipient's income
Life insurance death benefits: Generally excluded
Municipal bond interest: Excluded from federal tax
Scholarships: Excluded for tuition/fees only (not room/board)
Group-term life: Only first $50,000 excluded
Physical injury damages: Excluded; Punitive damages: Taxable
Foreign earned income: Up to $126,500 excluded (2024)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Gifts vs. Compensation: Test Your Knowledge

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah worked as a home health aide for Mrs. Gable for 5 years. Upon Mrs. Gable's death, Sarah received $50,000 from Mrs. Gable's will. In addition, Sarah's employer, ABC Home Healthcare, gave her a bonus of $1,000 in recognition of her years of service to Mrs. Gable. How much of these payments should Sarah include in her gross income?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The $50,000 inheritance from Mrs. Gable is excluded from gross income. However, the $1,000 bonus from ABC Home Healthcare is considered compensation for past services and is therefore included in gross income.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the difference between a true gift motivated by affection and a payment for services rendered.

reveal.question: What is the general tax treatment of life insurance proceeds received as a lump sum death benefit by a beneficiary?
reveal.answer: Generally, life insurance proceeds received as a lump sum death benefit are excluded from the beneficiary's gross income. However, this exclusion does not apply if the policy was transferred for valuable consideration, or in some cases for employer-owned life insurance.

sections.title: Inheritance vs. Income from Inherited Property

rows.label: Taxability
values: Generally Excluded from Gross Income
Generally Included in Gross Income

rows.label: Example
values: Receiving a house from a deceased relative
Renting out a house received from a deceased relative

title: 🆕 H.R. 1: Tip Income Exclusion
description: Understand the new tip income exclusion provisions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: NEW LAW ALERT! H.R. 1 (the current tax legislation) includes provisions for excluding certain tip income from taxation. This is new material that may appear on future exams—understand the basics and limitations!

sections.title: Overview of Tip Income Exclusion
sections.content:
**New provision for tip workers:**

• Certain cash tips may be excluded from income
• Designed to benefit service industry workers
• Subject to income limitations
• Temporary provision (sunset applies)

**Note:** This represents a significant change from prior law where ALL tips were taxable

sections.title: Eligible Tips
sections.content:
**Tips that may qualify:**

• Cash tips from customers
• Tips received in covered occupations
• Tips reported to employer

**Requirements:**
• Must be in qualifying service industry
• Income must be below threshold
• Must properly report tips

**Details may vary—check current guidance!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Tip Exclusion
sections.content:
**"TIPS"** for the exclusion:

**T**ip income eligible
**I**ncome limits apply
**P**roperly reported
**S**ervice industry workers

**New benefit for tip workers!**

sections.title: Income Limitations
sections.content:
**The exclusion phases out:**

• Begins phasing out at certain income levels
• Completely eliminated at higher incomes
• Designed for lower/middle-income workers

**Similar to other income-based benefits:**
• Those earning above thresholds don't qualify
• Check current thresholds as they may adjust

sections.title: Employer Considerations
sections.content:
**Employers still must:**

• Collect tip reports from employees
• Pay employer share of FICA on tips
• Report tips on W-2

**The exclusion affects INCOME tax, not necessarily employment taxes**

**Proper tip reporting remains essential!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Limited Application
sections.content:
**Important limitations:**

• NOT all tips are excluded
• Income thresholds apply
• Temporary provision (check sunset date)
• Employment taxes may still apply
• Must be properly reported tips

**Don't assume ALL tip income is now tax-free!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: H.R. 1 introduces new tip income exclusion
Designed to benefit service industry workers
Income limitations apply—not universal
Tips must still be properly reported
Employment taxes may still apply
Temporary provision with sunset date
Check current guidance for specific details

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Under the new H.R. 1 provision, which of the following statements is TRUE regarding the tip income exclusion?
options: All cash tips received by service industry workers are excludable from income, regardless of income level.
The tip income exclusion is a permanent provision that will continue indefinitely.
The tip income exclusion phases out at certain income levels, benefiting primarily lower to middle-income service industry workers.
Only tips that are NOT reported to the employer are eligible for the exclusion.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is that the tip income exclusion phases out at certain income levels, primarily benefiting lower to middle-income service industry workers. The exclusion is not permanent and is subject to a sunset provision. All eligible tips MUST be reported to the employer.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the income limitations and the reporting requirements of the tip income exclusion.

reveal.question: List the three key requirements for tips to qualify for the H.R. 1 tip income exclusion.
reveal.answer:
1. Must be in a qualifying service industry.
2. Income must be below the threshold.
3. Must properly report tips to the employer.

sections.title: Taxation of Tips: Before vs. After H.R. 1

rows.label: Taxability of Tips
values: All tips were taxable income.
Certain cash tips may be excluded from income, subject to limitations.

rows.label: Impact on Taxable Income
values: Tips increased taxable income directly.
Exclusion, if eligible, reduces taxable income.

rows.label: Income Limitations
values: No income limitations specifically related to tip taxation.
Exclusion phases out at higher income levels.

title: Above-the-Line Deductions
description: Master deductions for AGI that reduce adjusted gross income

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Above-the-line deductions are pure gold! They reduce AGI, which affects eligibility for many tax benefits. Unlike itemized deductions, everyone can take these regardless of whether they itemize. Know these cold for REG!

sections.title: What Are Above-the-Line Deductions?
sections.content:
**Deductions FOR AGI (not FROM AGI):**

**Gross Income**
- Above-the-line deductions
= **Adjusted Gross Income (AGI)**
- Standard OR Itemized deductions
- QBI deduction
= **Taxable Income**

**Above-the-line = Available to ALL taxpayers**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Above-the-Line
sections.content:
**"I SHAMES"** above-the-line:

**I**RA contributions
**S**tudent loan interest
**H**ealth insurance (self-employed)
**A**limony (pre-2019 agreements)
**M**oving (military only)
**E**ducator expenses
**S**elf-employment tax (50%)

**These reduce AGI!**

sections.title: IRA Deduction
sections.content:
**Traditional IRA contribution deduction:**

• Maximum: $7,000 ($8,000 if 50+) for 2024
• Must have earned income
• Deduction phases out if covered by employer plan AND income exceeds limits

**Phase-out (single, covered by plan):**
• Begins at $77,000 AGI
• Ends at $87,000 AGI

**Roth IRA:** No deduction (contributions with after-tax dollars)

sections.title: Student Loan Interest
sections.content:
**Deduction for interest paid:**

• Maximum: $2,500 per year
• Must be legally obligated to pay
• Loan for qualified education expenses

**Phase-out (2024):**
• Single: $80,000-$95,000
• MFJ: $165,000-$195,000

**Even if parent pays on child's loan, child gets deduction (if legally obligated)**

sections.title: Self-Employed Health Insurance
sections.content:
**100% deduction for:**

• Health insurance premiums
• Dental insurance
• Long-term care insurance
• Coverage for self, spouse, dependents

**Limitations:**
• Cannot exceed net self-employment income
• Cannot be eligible for employer-sponsored plan
• Applies to S corp >2% shareholders too

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: HSA Contributions
sections.content:
**Health Savings Account (HSA) contributions:**

• ABOVE-the-line deduction!
• 2024 limits: $4,150 (individual), $8,300 (family)
• Must have high-deductible health plan (HDHP)
• Catch-up: $1,000 additional if 55+

**Triple tax benefit:**
1. Deductible contribution
2. Tax-free growth
3. Tax-free qualified withdrawals

sections.title: Self-Employment Tax Deduction
sections.content:
**50% of self-employment tax is deductible:**

• SE tax = 15.3% (12.4% SS + 2.9% Medicare)
• Above-the-line deduction for employer-equivalent portion

**Calculation:**
• Net SE earnings × 92.35% × 15.3% = SE tax
• SE tax × 50% = Deduction

**Partially offsets extra tax burden on self-employed**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Above-the-line deductions reduce AGI (available to all)
IRA deduction: Up to $7,000 ($8,000 if 50+), income limits apply
Student loan interest: Up to $2,500, phases out at higher income
Self-employed health: 100% deductible (up to SE income)
50% of SE tax is deductible
HSA contributions: Above-the-line, triple tax benefit
Educator expenses: $300 ($600 MFJ)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah, single and 48 years old, contributes $7,000 to a Traditional IRA in 2024. She is covered by her employer's retirement plan. Her Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) is $82,000. What is the deductible amount of her Traditional IRA contribution?
options: Some amount between $0 and $7,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The IRA deduction phases out for single individuals covered by an employer plan with AGI between $77,000 and $87,000. The phase-out range is $10,000. Sarah's AGI is $5,000 above the starting point ($82,000 - $77,000). Therefore, her deduction is reduced by ($5,000 / $10,000) * $7,000 = $3,500. Her deductible amount is $7,000 - $3,500 = $3,500. Note that it will be some amount between $0 and $7,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the phase-out amount based on how much Sarah's AGI exceeds the starting point of the phase-out range.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating Adjusted Gross Income (AGI)?
reveal.answer: Gross Income - Above-the-Line Deductions = Adjusted Gross Income (AGI)

rows.label: Deductibility of Contributions
values: Deductible, subject to limitations and phase-outs
Not deductible

rows.label: Tax Treatment of Earnings
values: Tax-deferred growth
Tax-free growth

title: Standard vs Itemized Deductions
description: Compare standard deduction with itemized deductions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Every taxpayer must choose: Standard deduction or itemize? TCJA dramatically increased the standard deduction, making itemizing less common. Understanding what's deductible—and when to itemize—is essential for REG!

sections.title: When to Itemize
sections.content:
**Itemize when itemized deductions > standard deduction:**

**Common situations:**
• Large mortgage interest
• Significant charitable contributions
• Major medical expenses
• High state/local taxes (limited to $10K)

**Most taxpayers now take standard deduction** (post-TCJA)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Itemized Deductions
sections.content:
**"COMIC MATH"** to itemize:

**C**haritable contributions
**O**ther taxes (SALT—limited!)
**M**edical expenses (>7.5% AGI)
**I**nterest (mortgage, investment)
**C**asualty losses (federally declared disasters)

**M**ortgage interest is most common!
**A**ll have limits
**T**hreshold applies to medical
**H**igh-income? No overall limit anymore

sections.title: Medical Expenses
sections.content:
**Deductible if exceed 7.5% of AGI:**

**Deductible:**
• Doctor/hospital bills
• Prescription drugs
• Health insurance premiums
• Medical equipment
• Long-term care (limited)

**NOT deductible:**
• Cosmetic surgery (unless medically necessary)
• General health items (gym, vitamins)
• Premiums paid with pre-tax dollars

sections.title: State and Local Taxes (SALT)
sections.content:
**SALT deduction LIMITED to $10,000:**

**Includes:**
• State/local income taxes, OR
• State/local sales taxes (choose one)
• Real property taxes
• Personal property taxes

**$10,000 cap applies to total!**
($5,000 if MFS)

**Major TCJA limitation**

sections.title: Mortgage Interest
sections.content:
**Acquisition debt interest deductible:**

**Debt limits:**
• Mortgages after 12/15/17: $750,000
• Mortgages before 12/15/17: $1,000,000

**Home equity debt:**
• Interest NOT deductible (TCJA eliminated)
• Exception: If used for home improvement

**Must be primary or second home**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: SALT Cap Impact
sections.content:
**The $10,000 SALT cap is huge!**

**Example:**
• State income tax: $15,000
• Property tax: $8,000
• Total: $23,000
• **Deductible: Only $10,000**

**This cap pushes many to standard deduction!**

**MFS = $5,000 cap (even worse)**

sections.title: Charitable Contributions
sections.content:
**Cash donations:**
• Generally up to 60% of AGI

**Appreciated property:**
• Generally up to 30% of AGI
• Can elect 50% if use basis

**Requirements:**
• Qualified organization (501(c)(3))
• Written acknowledgment if $250+
• Appraisal if property >$5,000

**Excess carries forward 5 years**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 2024 standard deduction: $14,600 (S), $29,200 (MFJ)
Most taxpayers now use standard deduction
Medical expenses: Only amount exceeding 7.5% of AGI
SALT deduction: LIMITED to $10,000 ($5,000 MFS)
Mortgage interest: Limited to $750K acquisition debt (post-2017)
Home equity interest: No longer deductible (unless for home improvement)
Charitable: Generally 60% AGI limit for cash

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Mark and Sarah are filing jointly and have the following potential itemized deductions: $8,000 in mortgage interest, $3,000 in state and local taxes, and $1,000 in charitable contributions. Assuming the standard deduction for married filing jointly is $27,700 (2023), what should Mark and Sarah do?
options: Take the standard deduction.
Itemize their deductions.
Take the standard deduction and deduct the charitable contributions separately.
They are indifferent between itemizing and taking the standard deduction.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The total itemized deductions are $8,000 + $3,000 + $1,000 = $12,000. Since $12,000 is less than the standard deduction of $27,700, they should take the standard deduction. Taking the standard deduction will result in a lower tax liability.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Compare the total itemized deductions to the standard deduction amount.

reveal.question: What percentage of Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) must medical expenses exceed in order to be deductible?
reveal.answer: Medical expenses are deductible to the extent they exceed 7.5% of your Adjusted Gross Income (AGI).

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a deductible medical expense for individual income tax purposes?
options: Payments for prescription drugs.
Health insurance premiums.
The cost of cosmetic surgery recommended by a doctor to correct a congenital abnormality.
Monthly gym membership to maintain general health.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: General health items, like gym memberships and vitamins, are not deductible medical expenses. Cosmetic surgery is deductible if medically necessary to correct a congenital abnormality, disease, or injury.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the purpose of the expense - is it for treatment of a medical condition or general well-being?

title: Qualified Business Income Deduction (199A)
description: Master the 20% deduction for pass-through business income

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Section 199A deduction can be worth 20% of qualified business income! This TCJA provision helps pass-through entities compete with C corps' lower rates. But it's complex with multiple limitations—master this for REG!

sections.title: Basic QBI Deduction
sections.content:
**20% of Qualified Business Income:**

• Available to: Sole proprietors, S corp shareholders, partners
• Deduction is "below the line" (but not itemized)
• Does NOT reduce SE tax
• Reduces taxable income, not AGI

**Formula:** Lesser of:
1. 20% of QBI, or
2. 20% of taxable income (before QBI deduction)

sections.title: What is Qualified Business Income?
sections.content:
**QBI = Net income from qualified trade or business**

**Includes:**
• Business income
• Gains/losses from business
• Self-employment income

**Excludes:**
• W-2 wages earned as employee
• Investment income (interest, dividends, capital gains)
• Reasonable compensation from S corp
• Guaranteed payments to partners

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: QBI Components
sections.content:
**"QUALIFIED"** for QBI:

**Q**ualified trade or business
**U**nearned income NOT included
**A**ggregate all businesses
**L**imitations may apply
**I**nvestment income excluded
**F**rom pass-through entities
**I**ndividuals claim deduction
**E**mployee wages NOT QBI
**D**eduction up to 20%

sections.title: Specified Service Trade or Business (SSTB)
sections.content:
**SSTBs have limited deduction if income too high:**

**SSTB includes:**
• Health, law, accounting
• Consulting
• Athletics, performing arts
• Financial services, brokerage
• Any business where principal asset is reputation/skill

**NOT SSTB:** Engineering, architecture

sections.title: W-2 Wage and UBIA Limitations
sections.content:
**Above threshold, deduction limited to GREATER of:**

1. **50% of W-2 wages** paid by business, OR
2. **25% of W-2 wages + 2.5% of UBIA** (unadjusted basis immediately after acquisition of qualified property)

**UBIA = Depreciable property in business**

**Businesses with no employees or property may have limited deduction!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: SSTB Complete Phase-Out
sections.content:
**High-income SSTB owners get ZERO deduction!**

**Example:** Attorney with $450,000 taxable income
• SSTB = Yes (legal services)
• Income above threshold
• **QBI deduction = $0**

**Same attorney with $150,000 income:**
• Below threshold
• **Full 20% deduction available**

**SSTBs are completely phased out above threshold!**

sections.title: Calculation Steps
sections.content:
**Simplified approach:**

1. Calculate QBI for each business
2. Check if SSTB
3. Compare income to threshold
4. Apply W-2/UBIA limitation if needed
5. Calculate 20% of QBI (limited)
6. Apply overall taxable income limit

**Many taxpayers below threshold get simple 20%**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: QBI deduction: Up to 20% of qualified business income
Available to pass-through entity owners and sole proprietors
W-2 wages, investment income, guaranteed payments NOT QBI
SSTB limitation: Phase-out and complete denial at high income
Above threshold: W-2 wage and UBIA limitations apply
Engineering and architecture are NOT SSTBs
Deduction limited to 20% of taxable income

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: John, a single taxpayer, owns a qualified business that generates $80,000 in QBI. His taxable income before the QBI deduction is $30,000. What is John's QBI deduction?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The QBI deduction is the lesser of 20% of QBI or 20% of taxable income. 20% of QBI ($80,000) = $16,000. 20% of taxable income ($30,000) = $6,000. However, the problem states his taxable income is *before* the QBI deduction, so you must consider the impact of the QBI deduction when calculating taxable income. The answer is 20% of $80,000 = $16,000. The correct answer is C, $24,000 (20% of QBI).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate both 20% of QBI and 20% of taxable income before the QBI deduction, and then choose the lesser of the two.

reveal.question: List three items that are specifically excluded from the calculation of Qualified Business Income (QBI).
reveal.answer: 1. W-2 wages earned as an employee. 2. Investment income (interest, dividends, capital gains). 3. Reasonable compensation from an S corporation.

rows.label: Impact on Taxable Income
values: Reduces taxable income.
Deduction for one-half of self-employment tax reduces AGI, but the tax itself is in addition to income tax.

rows.label: Relationship to AGI
values: Reduces taxable income, but not AGI directly (it's a below-the-line deduction but not an itemized deduction).
Deduction for one-half of self-employment tax reduces AGI.

rows.label: Calculation Base
values: Based on 20% of QBI or 20% of taxable income (before QBI deduction), whichever is lower.
Based on 92.35% of self-employment income.

title: 🆕 H.R. 1: SALT Deduction Changes
description: Understand changes to the state and local tax deduction

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The SALT cap has been one of the most controversial TCJA provisions! H.R. 1 addresses changes to the state and local tax deduction limits. Understanding these changes is important for advising clients in high-tax states!

sections.title: Current SALT Cap Review
sections.content:
**TCJA imposed $10,000 limit:**

• State and local income taxes
• State and local sales taxes (alternative)
• Real property taxes
• Personal property taxes

**Combined total capped at $10,000**
($5,000 if married filing separately)

**Dramatically impacted high-tax state residents**

sections.title: H.R. 1 SALT Provisions
sections.content:
**Potential changes under H.R. 1:**

• Modifications to the $10,000 cap
• Possible increase in limit
• Alternative calculation methods
• Continued limitation approach

**Note:** Check current law for specific changes enacted. Provisions may have been modified during legislative process.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: SALT Components
sections.content:
**"SALT"** stands for:

**S**tate income tax
**A**nd
**L**ocal taxes (property, sales)
**T**otal capped!

**All combined toward the limit**

sections.title: Impact on High-Tax States
sections.content:
**States most affected by SALT cap:**

• New York
• California
• New Jersey
• Connecticut
• Maryland

**These states have:**
• High income tax rates
• High property values/taxes
• Many residents hitting the cap

sections.title: State Workarounds
sections.content:
**States attempted workarounds:**

• Pass-through entity (PTE) taxes
• Charitable contribution programs
• Entity-level taxes (some upheld by IRS)

**PTE workaround:**
• Entity pays state tax (deductible at entity level)
• Owners get credit/exclusion
• Effectively circumvents individual SALT cap

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: MFS Penalty
sections.content:
**Married Filing Separately = WORSE cap!**

• MFS SALT limit: $5,000 (not $10,000)
• Cannot claim standard deduction if spouse itemizes

**Planning:** High-tax couples should typically file jointly

**The SALT cap particularly penalizes MFS filers!**

sections.title: Legislative Considerations
sections.content:
**Policy debates around SALT:**

• Critics say cap penalizes high-tax states
• Supporters say it's fair (reduces subsidy to high-tax states)
• Temporary provision (original sunset 2025)
• H.R. 1 addresses extension/modification

**Stay current on final legislation!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SALT cap currently $10,000 ($5,000 MFS)
Includes state income, sales, and property taxes
H.R. 1 may modify this limitation
High-tax state residents most affected
PTE workarounds provide some relief
MFS filers face worse $5,000 limit
Check current law for final provisions

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Under current law, a married couple filing jointly with $12,000 in state income taxes, $3,000 in real property taxes, and $1,000 in personal property taxes can deduct what amount for SALT purposes?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The TCJA imposed a $10,000 limit on the total SALT deduction. Even though the couple's total state and local taxes are $16,000, they are limited to deducting $10,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the overall limit.

reveal.question: List the four types of taxes that are included in the SALT deduction calculation under current law.
reveal.answer: The four types of taxes included are: state and local income taxes, state and local sales taxes (as an alternative to income taxes), real property taxes, and personal property taxes.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following states is MOST likely to be significantly impacted by the SALT deduction limit?
options: Texas
Florida
New York
Tennessee
knowledgeCheck.explanation: New York is a high-tax state with high property values and income tax rates. Residents are more likely to be significantly impacted by the $10,000 SALT deduction limit than residents of Texas, Florida, or Tennessee, which have lower or no state income taxes.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which states have high income and property taxes.

title: Filing Status Determination
description: Master the rules for determining correct filing status

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Filing status affects tax brackets, standard deduction, and eligibility for credits! Choosing the wrong status can cost thousands or trigger IRS penalties. Know the rules for each status—especially Head of Household, which is frequently tested!

sections.title: Married Filing Jointly (MFJ)
sections.content:
**Requirements:**

• Married on December 31 of tax year
• Both spouses agree to file jointly

**Advantages:**
• Highest standard deduction
• Widest tax brackets
• Most credits available

**Disadvantage:** Joint and several liability for tax

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Head of Household
sections.content:
**"HOMES"** test for HOH:

**H**ome—Maintained for more than half year
**O**ccupied by qualifying person (generally)
**M**arriage—Must be unmarried (or considered unmarried)
**E**xpense—Paid more than half cost of home
**S**omeone qualified lives with you

**HOH = Unmarried + Maintain Home + Qualifying Person**

sections.title: Head of Household Requirements
sections.content:
**Must meet ALL:**

1. **Unmarried** (or "considered unmarried")
2. **Paid more than half** home costs
3. **Qualifying person** lived with you more than half year

**Qualifying persons:**
• Qualifying child
• Qualifying relative who is close family member

**Exception:** Dependent parent doesn't have to live with you

sections.title: Considered Unmarried Rule
sections.content:
**Married but treated as unmarried if:**
