In exchange, Alice receives 50 shares of stock, Bob receives 30 shares, and Carol receives 20 shares. Which of the following is true regarding the tax consequences of this transaction?
options: The transaction qualifies as a tax-free exchange under Section 351.
Alice and Bob will recognize a gain, but Carol will not.
Only Alice's transfer qualifies for Section 351 treatment.
The transaction does not qualify as a tax-free exchange under Section 351 because the transferors do not control the corporation immediately after the exchange.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is D. To qualify for Section 351, the transferors must control (80% ownership) the corporation immediately after the exchange. Carol's contribution of services does not count toward the 80% control requirement. Therefore, only Alice and Bob's shares are considered (50 + 30 = 80 shares). Since they do not hold at least 80% of the shares without Carol, the transaction does not qualify under Section 351.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that services are not considered 'property' for Section 351 purposes and do not count toward the 80% control test.

reveal.question: In a Section 351 exchange, if boot is received, what is the maximum amount of gain that a transferor will recognize?
reveal.answer: The transferor will recognize gain up to the LESSER of (1) the gain realized on the transfer, or (2) the amount of boot received. Losses are never recognized in a Section 351 exchange.

rows.label: Definition
values: Tangible or intangible assets, including cash, accounts receivable, patents, and equipment.
Performance of tasks or duties for another party, including legal, accounting, or consulting services.

rows.label: Impact on 80% Control
values: Counts towards the 80% control requirement.
Does not count towards the 80% control requirement.

rows.label: Tax Treatment
values: Transfer is potentially tax-free if all Section 351 requirements are met.
Receipt of stock for services is always taxable as ordinary income.

title: C Corporation Taxable Income
description: Understand corporate taxable income and rates

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: C corporations are separate taxable entities paying their own tax! Understanding taxable income calculations, the flat 21% rate, and special deductions like DRD is essential for REG and corporate tax practice!

sections.title: Corporate Tax Rate
sections.content:
**Flat 21% rate (post-TCJA)**

**Corporate taxable income:**

Gross income
− Deductions
= Taxable income × 21%

**No graduated rates—just flat 21%**

sections.title: Key Corporate Deductions
rows: Applies to pass-through, not C corps
Limited to 30% of adjusted taxable income

sections.title: Dividends Received Deduction (DRD)
sections.content:
**Deduction for dividends from other domestic corporations:**

• **<20% ownership:** 50% DRD
• **20-79% ownership:** 65% DRD
• **80%+ ownership:** 100% DRD (affiliated group)

**Purpose:** Avoid triple taxation on corporate income

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: DRD Percentages
sections.content:
**"50-65-100"** for DRD:

**50%** DRD = Less than 20% owned
**65%** DRD = 20% to 79% owned
**100%** DRD = 80%+ owned (affiliated)

**More ownership = More deduction!**

sections.title: DRD Limitation
sections.content:
**DRD limited to taxable income:**

• Cannot create or increase NOL
• Exception: If full DRD would create NOL, take full DRD

**Calculation:**
Lesser of:
• Dividends × DRD %
• Taxable income (before DRD) × DRD %

sections.title: Organizational vs. Start-Up Costs
sections.content:
**Organizational costs (forming corp):**
• Deduct up to $5,000 (reduced if costs exceed $50K)
• Amortize remainder over 180 months

**Start-up costs (investigating/starting business):**
• Same: $5,000 deduction + 180-month amortization

**Examples:** Legal fees, incorporation fees, accounting setup

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Charitable Contribution Timing
sections.content:
**Accrual method corps can accrue contributions:**

• Board authorizes by year-end
• Payment made by 15th day of 4th month
• Election to treat as current year

**Still limited to 10% of taxable income!**

**Calculate 10% limit BEFORE DRD and charitable deduction**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Flat 21% corporate tax rate (TCJA)
DRD: 50%/65%/100% based on ownership percentage
DRD limited to taxable income (unless creates NOL)
Charitable contributions: 10% limit
Organizational/start-up: $5K deduction + 180-month amortization
NOL deduction limited to 80% of taxable income
Interest expense may be limited (§163(j))

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corporation has gross income of $500,000 and allowable deductions of $150,000. What is XYZ Corporation's income tax liability?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Taxable income is calculated as Gross Income - Deductions = $500,000 - $150,000 = $350,000. The tax liability is calculated as Taxable Income * 21% = $350,000 * 0.21 = $73,500.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the corporate tax rate is a flat rate.

reveal.question: What is the Dividends Received Deduction (DRD) percentage for a corporation that owns 30% of another domestic corporation's stock?
reveal.answer: The DRD percentage is 65%. This applies to ownership between 20% and 79%.

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Corporation owns 15% of DEF Corporation's stock. DEF Corporation paid a dividend of $100,000 to ABC Corporation. ABC Corporation's taxable income, before the dividends received deduction, is $300,000. What is ABC Corporation's taxable income after considering the dividends received deduction?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: ABC Corporation is eligible for a 50% DRD on the $100,000 dividend, resulting in a deduction of $50,000. Taxable income is $300,000 (before DRD) - $50,000 (DRD) = $250,000. However, the DRD is limited to 50% of taxable income before the DRD. 50% of $300,000 is $150,000, so the DRD is not limited. The taxable income becomes $300,000 - $50,000 = $250,000. DRD limit applies to situations where DRD would generate a loss.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the DRD amount and consider any limitations based on taxable income.

title: C Corporation Distributions & E&P
description: Master dividend and non-dividend distributions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Corporate distributions can be dividends, return of capital, or capital gains! Understanding the E&P ordering rules determines how shareholders are taxed. This is a key REG topic for corporate transactions!

sections.title: Distribution Ordering Rules
sections.content:
**Distributions are taxed in order:**

1. **Dividend:** To extent of E&P (current + accumulated)
2. **Return of capital:** Reduces stock basis (tax-free)
3. **Capital gain:** Excess after basis reduced to zero

**E&P is key to determining dividend treatment!**

sections.title: Earnings & Profits (E&P)
sections.content:
**E&P = Corporate ability to pay dividends:**

**Starts with taxable income, then adjust:**

+ Tax-exempt income
+ DRD (add back)
+ Federal income tax (subtract)
+ Life insurance proceeds (add)
− Life insurance premiums (subtract)
− Penalties and fines (subtract)
+ Excess depreciation (timing)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: E&P Adjustments
sections.content:
**"ADD BACK FAKE"** benefits:

**A**dd municipal bond interest
**D**RD added back
**D**epreciation (use ADS)

**"SUBTRACT REAL"** payments:

**S**ubtract federal income tax
**U**se actual depreciation (ADS)
**B**ad deductions (penalties, etc.)

**E&P = Economic earnings, not tax earnings**

sections.title: Current vs. Accumulated E&P
rows: Dividend to extent of current first

sections.title: Property Distributions
sections.content:
**Corporation distributes property (not cash):**

**Corporation recognizes gain:**
• FMV > Basis = Gain recognized
• FMV < Basis = NO loss recognized

**Shareholder receives:**
• Amount distributed = FMV of property
• Shareholder's basis = FMV

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Appreciated Property
sections.content:
**Corporation recognizes gain on appreciated property distributions!**

**Example:**
• Corp distributes land: FMV $100K, Basis $40K
• Corp recognizes $60K gain
• E&P increases by $60K gain
• Distribution amount = $100K FMV

**Corp can't avoid gain by distributing property!**

sections.title: Constructive Dividends
sections.content:
**Disguised dividends (taxable to shareholder):**

• Excess compensation to shareholder-employee
• Below-market rent to shareholder
• Loans to shareholders with no intent to repay
• Personal expenses paid by corp

**IRS recharacterizes as dividends**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Distribution order: Dividend → Return of capital → Capital gain
E&P determines dividend amount
Current E&P used first, then accumulated E&P
Property distribution: Corp recognizes gain (FMV > Basis)
Shareholder's basis in distributed property = FMV
Constructive dividends: Disguised benefits taxable
No loss on distribution of depreciated property

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corporation has accumulated E&P of $50,000 and current E&P of $20,000. Shareholder John receives a distribution of $90,000. How will this distribution be taxed to John?
options: $70,000 dividend, $20,000 return of capital, $0 capital gain
$50,000 dividend, $40,000 return of capital, $0 capital gain
$70,000 dividend, $0 return of capital, $20,000 capital gain
$90,000 dividend, $0 return of capital, $0 capital gain
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The distribution is first taxed as a dividend to the extent of both current and accumulated E&P ($50,000 + $20,000 = $70,000). The remaining $20,000 ($90,000 - $70,000) is taxed as a capital gain since we are assuming John's basis is already reduced to zero.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the order: Dividend (to extent of E&P), Return of Capital, then Capital Gain.

reveal.question: Name three items that are added back to taxable income when calculating Earnings & Profits (E&P).
reveal.answer: Three items added back to taxable income when calculating E&P are: 1. Tax-exempt income, 2. Dividends Received Deduction (DRD), and 3. Life insurance proceeds.

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Corporation has taxable income of $100,000. It also has $10,000 in tax-exempt interest income, received a $20,000 dividends received deduction, paid $25,000 in federal income taxes, received $50,000 in life insurance proceeds (policy on a key employee), and paid $5,000 in life insurance premiums. What is ABC Corporation's E&P?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: E&P is calculated as follows: $100,000 (Taxable Income) + $10,000 (Tax-Exempt Interest) + $20,000 (DRD) - $25,000 (Federal Income Tax) + $50,000 (Life Insurance Proceeds) - $5,000 (Life Insurance Premiums) = $150,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Carefully consider which items increase and decrease E&P. Remember that life insurance premiums are subtracted, but proceeds are added.

title: Accumulated Earnings Tax
description: Understand the penalty tax on excess retained earnings

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: C corporations can't just accumulate earnings to avoid shareholder-level tax! The Accumulated Earnings Tax (AET) penalizes improper retention. Know this anti-abuse provision for REG!

sections.title: Accumulated Earnings Tax (AET)
sections.content:
**20% penalty tax on:**

Accumulated taxable income

**Applies when:**
• Corporation accumulates earnings
• Beyond reasonable business needs
• To avoid shareholder dividend tax

**Intent to avoid tax is key!**

sections.title: AET Credit
sections.content:
**Accumulated earnings credit:**

**General corporations:**
• Greater of: $250,000 OR reasonable needs

**Personal service corporations:**
• Greater of: $150,000 OR reasonable needs

**Reasonable needs:**
• Expansion plans
• Debt retirement
• Working capital

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: AET
sections.content:
**"AVOID TAX = 20% TAX"**

**A**ccumulate beyond needs
**V**oid of business purpose
**O**nly for shareholder benefit
**I**ntent to dodge dividends
**D**ouble tax now!

**AET = 20% on excess accumulations**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Intent Requirement
sections.content:
**AET requires tax avoidance intent!**

• Burden of proof on IRS initially
• Shifts to taxpayer if prima facie case
• Document business needs thoroughly

**Unlike PHC tax (mechanical), AET is subjective!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: AET: 20% on earnings accumulated to avoid dividend tax
Credit: $250,000 (or $150,000 for PSC) or reasonable needs
Intent to avoid tax is required element
Document reasonable business needs carefully
Excess accumulations encourage dividend distributions

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Corporation, a general corporation, has accumulated earnings of $300,000. The IRS determines that $50,000 of these earnings are reasonably needed for bona fide expansion plans. Assuming the IRS assesses the accumulated earnings tax, what amount of accumulated taxable income is subject to the 20% penalty?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The accumulated earnings credit for a general corporation is the greater of $250,000 or reasonable needs. In this case, $250,000 is greater than the $50,000 of reasonable needs. Therefore, the entire $300,000 is sheltered by the $250,000 credit, and the $50,000 for expansion, resulting in $0 taxable income subject to the tax.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the accumulated earnings credit for general corporations.

reveal.question: What is the tax rate for the Accumulated Earnings Tax?
reveal.answer: The Accumulated Earnings Tax is levied at a flat rate of 20% on accumulated taxable income.

rows.label: Credit Calculation
values: Greater of $250,000 or Reasonable Needs
Greater of $150,000 or Reasonable Needs

title: Personal Holding Company Tax
description: Understand the PHC penalty tax on passive income

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Personal Holding Company (PHC) tax targets closely held corporations with passive income! Unlike AET, this is a mechanical test—no intent required. Know these rules for REG!

sections.title: PHC Two-Part Test
sections.content:
**Closely held corporation with passive income:**

**Two tests:**
1. **Stock ownership:** >50% owned by 5 or fewer individuals
2. **Income:** ≥60% from PHC income (passive)

**Both tests must be met!**

sections.title: PHC Income
sections.content:
**PHC income includes:**

• Dividends, interest, rents, royalties
• Annuities
• Personal service contracts (named individual)
• Income from estates/trusts

**Rents may escape if >50% of ordinary income**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: PHC Tests
sections.content:
**"5-50-60"** for PHC:

**5** or fewer individuals
**50**% stock ownership (more than)
**60**% passive income (at least)

**Meet both = PHC tax!**

sections.title: Deficiency Dividend
sections.content:
**Relief from PHC tax:**

• Corporation can pay "deficiency dividend"
• Paid after IRS determination
• Within 90 days of determination
• Eliminates or reduces PHC tax

**Not available for AET (can't cure intent retroactively)**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: AET vs. PHC
sections.content:
**Both are 20% but different triggers:**

**AET:**
• Intent to avoid tax (subjective)
• Any corporation can be subject
• Credit for reasonable needs

**PHC:**
• Mechanical tests (ownership + income)
• Closely held + 60% passive income
• No intent required

**Deficiency dividend works for PHC only!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: PHC: Closely held (>50% by 5 or fewer) + 60% passive income
PHC tax: 20% on undistributed PHC income
Mechanical test—no intent required (unlike AET)
Deficiency dividend can eliminate PHC tax
Distribute as dividends to avoid PHC tax

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Personal Holding Company Status: Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corp. is owned 60% by two individuals. XYZ Corp.'s adjusted ordinary gross income is $200,000. It has dividend income of $50,000, rental income of $70,000, and business income of $80,000. Is XYZ Corp. a personal holding company?
options: Yes, because the stock ownership test and the income test are met.
No, because the rental income is more than 50% of adjusted ordinary gross income.
No, because the business income exceeds the dividend income.
Yes, because the dividend income is more than 25% of adjusted ordinary gross income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: XYZ Corp. meets the stock ownership test (>50% owned by 5 or fewer individuals). The PHC income test is also met because PHC income ($50,000 dividends + $70,000 rents) is $120,000, which is greater than or equal to 60% of adjusted ordinary gross income ($200,000 x 60% = $120,000). Although rents can escape PHC income, they only do so if they constitute >50% of *ordinary income* and other PHC income is limited to 10% or less of ordinary income. In this case, the prompt does not specify *adjusted ordinary gross income* which is required to determine if the rent income escapes PHC treatment.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to analyze both the stock ownership and income tests. Consider how rents are treated.

reveal.question: List four types of income that are considered Personal Holding Company (PHC) income.
reveal.answer: Dividends, interest, rents, royalties, annuities, personal service contracts (named individual), and income from estates/trusts are all considered PHC income.

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Corp has 4 shareholders. Alice owns 20%, Bob owns 15%, Carol owns 10%, and David owns 6%. The remaining 49% is held by unrelated public shareholders. ABC Corp. has the following income: $100,000 in gross income from services, $40,000 in dividend income, and $70,000 in rental income. Can ABC Corp. be classified as a personal holding company?
options: Yes, ABC Corp. is a personal holding company because the PHC income exceeds 60% of adjusted ordinary gross income.
No, ABC Corp. is not a personal holding company because the stock ownership test is not met.
Yes, ABC Corp. is a personal holding company because the rental income is more than 50% of the total income.
No, ABC Corp. is not a personal holding company because the service income exceeds the dividend income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The stock ownership test requires that more than 50% of the stock is owned by 5 or fewer individuals. Alice, Bob, Carol, and David collectively own 51% (20% + 15% + 10% + 6%) of the stock. The adjusted ordinary gross income is $210,000 ($100,000 + $40,000 + $70,000). The PHC income is $110,000 ($40,000 + $70,000). 60% of $210,000 is $126,000. Because $110,000 is less than $126,000, the income test is *not* met. However, the stock ownership test *is* met. The question asks if ABC Corp *can* be classified as a PHC. Since both tests must be met, and the income test is not met, ABC Corp. cannot be classified as a PHC. Therefore the correct answer is that ABC Corp. is *not* a personal holding company because the stock ownership test is not met. Note: the adjusted ordinary gross income should have been specified, but the general ordinary gross income is sufficient to calculate the answer.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Carefully calculate the stock ownership percentage and the PHC income percentage of adjusted ordinary gross income. Remember that *both* tests must be met.

title: S Corporation Eligibility & Election
description: Master S corporation eligibility and election rules

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: S corporations provide pass-through taxation with liability protection! But strict eligibility rules apply—violate them and lose S status. Understanding requirements and election procedures is essential for REG!

sections.title: S Corporation Requirements
sections.content:
**To qualify as S corp:**

• Domestic corporation
• No more than 100 shareholders
• Only eligible shareholders
• One class of stock
• Not an ineligible corporation

**Fail any requirement = Lose S status!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: S Corp Requirements
sections.content:
**"DISCO"** for S corp:

**D**omestic corporation
**I**ndividuals (eligible shareholders)
**S**ingle class of stock
**C**ount (100 or fewer shareholders)
**O**ne election (Form 2553)

**DISCO = S corp eligibility!**

sections.title: One Class of Stock
sections.content:
**Only ONE class allowed:**

• All shares have identical distribution rights
• All shares have identical liquidation rights

**Allowed differences:**
• Voting vs. non-voting (still one class)

**Not allowed:**
• Preferred stock
• Different dividend preferences

sections.title: Election Timing
sections.content:
**Form 2553:**

**For election effective current year:**
• By 15th day of 3rd month (March 15 for calendar year)
• All shareholders must consent

**Late election:**
• Can file Form 2553 late with reasonable cause
• IRS may grant relief

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Family as One Shareholder
sections.content:
**Family members = One shareholder:**

• Spouse, children, grandchildren, parents
• Counts as single shareholder for 100-shareholder limit

**Example:**
• Parent + spouse + 3 children = 1 shareholder
• This allows more actual people to own S corp

**Family attribution rule helps stay under 100!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: S corp: Pass-through taxation with corporate liability protection
Requirements: 100 shareholders, one class stock, eligible shareholders
Eligible: Individuals, estates, certain trusts (not corps or partnerships)
Election: Form 2553 by 15th day of 3rd month
All shareholders must consent to election
Family members count as one shareholder

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following would disqualify a corporation from making an S corporation election?
options: Having 95 shareholders, all of whom are U.S. citizens.
Having both voting and non-voting common stock outstanding.
Having a shareholder who is a partnership.
Deriving 70% of its gross receipts from foreign sources.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: S corporations are limited to only eligible shareholders. Partnerships are not considered eligible shareholders. Therefore, having a shareholder who is a partnership would disqualify the corporation from making an S corporation election.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the types of entities that can be shareholders in an S corp.

reveal.question: What are the two rights that must be identical among all shares of stock in an S corporation to satisfy the 'one class of stock' requirement?
reveal.answer: All shares must have identical distribution rights and identical liquidation rights.

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corporation has two classes of stock outstanding: Common Stock and Preferred Stock. The Preferred Stock receives a guaranteed annual dividend before any dividends are paid to the Common Stock holders. All other rights are identical. Can XYZ Corporation elect S corporation status?
options: Yes, because the dividend preference does not violate the one-class-of-stock rule.
Yes, because all other rights besides the dividend are identical.
No, because the different dividend preferences create two classes of stock.
Yes, because the preferred stock is not considered stock for S corporation purposes.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The guaranteed annual dividend to Preferred Stock holders creates a different dividend preference, violating the one-class-of-stock rule. Therefore, XYZ Corporation cannot elect S corporation status.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the impact of the dividend preference on the one-class-of-stock rule.

title: S Corporation Shareholder Basis
description: Master basis calculations for S corporation shareholders

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Shareholder basis determines how much loss can be deducted and whether distributions are taxable! S corp basis rules differ from partnerships. Getting this right is critical for REG!

sections.title: Initial Basis
sections.content:
**Starting basis:**

• Cash contributed
• Property basis (not FMV)
• Services = FMV as ordinary income

**If formed under §351:**
• Same rules as C corp formation

sections.title: Basis Adjustment Order
sections.content:
**Annual adjustments (in order):**

1. **Increase:** Share of income items
2. **Increase:** Separately stated income
3. **Decrease:** Distributions
4. **Decrease:** Non-deductible expenses
5. **Decrease:** Separately stated losses/deductions
6. **Decrease:** Nonseparately stated loss

**Order matters for loss limitation!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Basis Adjustments
sections.content:
**"IID NN"** for basis order:

**I**ncome increases first
**I**ncome (separately stated) increases
**D**istributions decrease

**N**on-deductible decreases
**N**onseparately stated loss decreases

**Income UP first, then expenses DOWN**

sections.title: Debt Basis Rules
sections.content:
**S corp debt basis:**

• Only SHAREHOLDER loans to S corp
• Third-party debt does NOT create basis
• Different from partnerships!

**When losses exceed stock basis:**
• Can use debt basis
• Reduces debt basis
• Future income restores debt basis first, then stock

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Third-Party Debt
sections.content:
**S corps: Third-party debt = NO basis!**

**Unlike partnerships:**
• Partnership: Recourse/nonrecourse debt adds to basis
• S corp: Only shareholder loans add to basis

**Example:**
• S corp borrows $100K from bank
• Shareholders get NO basis increase
• Even if shareholder guarantees the loan!

**S corp debt basis = Shareholder loans ONLY**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Stock basis: Start with investment + adjustments
Order: Income first → Distributions → Losses
Debt basis: Only direct shareholder loans (not third-party)
Third-party debt gives NO basis (unlike partnerships)
Losses limited to stock basis + debt basis
Income restores debt basis before stock basis

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Barry contributed land with a basis of $40,000 and a fair market value of $60,000, and $10,000 cash, for a 60% ownership interest in an S corporation in a transaction qualifying under §351. He also performed services valued at $5,000 for the corporation. What is Barry's initial basis in his S corporation stock, assuming no liabilities were transferred to the corporation?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Barry's initial basis is calculated as follows: Land basis ($40,000) + Cash ($10,000) + FMV of services (which are taxable as ordinary income) ($5,000) = $55,000. The fair market value of the land is not relevant for basis calculation.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that services contributed are included at their FMV, and they are also taxable as ordinary income.

reveal.question: List the order in which S corporation shareholder basis is adjusted annually.
reveal.answer: 1. Increase: Share of income items; 2. Increase: Separately stated income; 3. Decrease: Distributions; 4. Decrease: Non-deductible expenses; 5. Decrease: Separately stated losses/deductions; 6. Decrease: Nonseparately stated loss.  The order matters because losses are only deductible to the extent of basis.

knowledgeCheck.question: At the beginning of the year, Karen had a basis of $10,000 in her S corporation stock. During the year, the S corporation allocated her $4,000 of ordinary income, $2,000 of charitable contributions, and $15,000 of ordinary loss. What amount of ordinary loss can Karen deduct this year?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Karen's basis is first increased by her share of income items ($4,000). Her basis is now $14,000. Next, the basis is decreased by separately stated deductions (charitable contributions of $2,000), making her basis $12,000. Therefore, she can only deduct $12,000 of her $15,000 ordinary loss. The remaining $3,000 loss is suspended and carried forward.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to adjust the basis for income items *before* deducting any losses!

title: S Corporation Built-In Gains Tax
description: Understand the tax on C-to-S conversion gains

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: When a C corporation converts to S status, built-in gains can't escape corporate tax! The BIG tax ensures C corp appreciation is taxed at the corporate level. Understanding this anti-abuse rule is important for REG!

sections.title: Built-In Gains (BIG) Tax Overview
sections.content:
**Corporate-level tax on:**

• Appreciation that existed when C corp became S corp
• Recognized during the recognition period
• At corporate rate (21%)

**Purpose:** Prevent avoidance of C corp tax through conversion

sections.title: Recognition Period
sections.content:
**5-year recognition period:**

• Starts when S election effective
• BIG tax applies to dispositions within 5 years
• After 5 years, no BIG tax

**Net recognized built-in gain:**
• Lesser of: Built-in gain OR taxable income

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: BIG Tax
sections.content:
**"5 YEARS TO ESCAPE"**

**5** years recognition period
**Y**ou pay corporate tax on BIG
**E**lection date sets "snapshot"
**A**ll appreciation at conversion date
**R**ecognized = Taxable
**S**ells trigger BIG (within 5 years)

**Wait 5 years = Escape BIG tax!**

sections.title: Calculating BIG Tax
rows: Built-in gain = FMV − Basis at conversion

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Applies to Former C Corps Only
sections.content:
**BIG tax ONLY applies if:**

• Previously was C corporation
• Converted to S corporation
• Has built-in gains at conversion

**Does NOT apply:**
• Always been S corp
• S corp acquiring C corp assets (different rules)

**BIG tax = C-to-S conversion issue**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: BIG tax: 21% corporate tax on built-in gains
Applies when C corp converts to S corp
5-year recognition period from conversion
Built-in gain = FMV at conversion − Basis
Only affects former C corporations
Avoid by holding assets 5+ years post-conversion

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Corp, a C corporation, elects S corporation status effective January 1, Year 1. At the time of the election, ABC Corp owned land with a fair market value of $500,000 and an adjusted basis of $200,000. In Year 3, ABC Corp sells the land for $600,000. What amount of gain, if any, is subject to the built-in gains tax?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The built-in gain is the appreciation that existed when the C corp became an S corp. This is calculated as FMV at conversion ($500,000) less adjusted basis at conversion ($200,000) = $300,000. The sale occurred within the 5-year recognition period. While the total gain on sale is $400,000 ($600,000 - $200,000), only the $300,000 appreciation at conversion is subject to the BIG tax.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the appreciation that occurred *before* the S election.

reveal.question: What is the duration of the recognition period for the built-in gains tax?
reveal.answer: The recognition period is 5 years, beginning when the S election becomes effective. Dispositions within this 5-year period can trigger the built-in gains tax if there was unrealized appreciation at the time of S election.

knowledgeCheck.question: An S corporation has a built-in gain of $75,000 and taxable income of $60,000 (before considering the built-in gain). What is the net recognized built-in gain?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The net recognized built-in gain is the *lesser* of the built-in gain or taxable income (before considering the built-in gain). In this case, taxable income ($60,000) is less than the built-in gain ($75,000), so the net recognized built-in gain is $60,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to compare two amounts and select the *smaller* one.

title: S Corporation Distributions & AAA
description: Master the Accumulated Adjustments Account

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: AAA tracks previously taxed S corp income! When an S corp has C corp E&P (from conversion), AAA determines whether distributions are tax-free or dividends. Understanding AAA ordering is critical for REG!

sections.title: What is AAA?
sections.content:
**Accumulated Adjustments Account:**

• Tracks previously taxed S corp income
• Similar to basis, but at corporate level
• Critical when S corp has C corp E&P

**AAA = "Tax-free" distribution pool**

sections.title: AAA Adjustments
sections.content:
**Increase AAA:**
• Separately stated income
• Nonseparately stated income

**Decrease AAA:**
• Separately stated deductions/losses
• Nonseparately stated deductions/losses
• Distributions
• Non-deductible expenses (not related to tax-exempt income)

**Tax-exempt income: Increases OAA, not AAA**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: AAA vs. Basis
sections.content:
**"AAA = ALREADY TAXED"**

**A**ccumulated S corp income
**A**lready passed through to shareholders
**A**vailable for tax-free distribution

**Key difference from basis:**
• AAA can go negative
• Basis cannot go below zero

sections.title: No E&P = Simpler Rules
sections.content:
**If S corp has NO C corp E&P:**

• Distributions first reduce basis
• Excess is capital gain
• AAA tracking still occurs but less critical

**AAA ordering critical only when C corp E&P exists!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: AAA Can Go Negative
sections.content:
**Unlike shareholder basis, AAA can be negative!**

**Example:**
• AAA: $50,000
• Losses: $80,000
• New AAA: ($30,000)

**Impact:**
• Negative AAA = No tax-free distributions from AAA
• Future income restores AAA before distributions

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: AAA tracks previously taxed S corp income
Distributions from AAA are tax-free (reduce basis)
C corp E&P distributed as dividends (after AAA)
AAA can go negative (unlike basis)
Distribution order: AAA → E&P → Basis → Capital gain
No E&P = Simpler rules (basis reduction only)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: During the current year, an S corporation has the following items: Tax-exempt interest income of $10,000, nonseparately stated income of $50,000, and non-deductible expenses (not related to tax-exempt income) of $5,000. Assuming the S corporation has sufficient AAA, what is the net effect of these items on the Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA)?
options: Increase of $60,000
Increase of $45,000
Increase of $50,000
Increase of $55,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The AAA is increased by nonseparately stated income ($50,000) and decreased by non-deductible expenses not related to tax-exempt income ($5,000). Tax-exempt interest income increases the Other Adjustments Account (OAA), not the AAA. Therefore, the net effect on AAA is an increase of $45,000 ($50,000 - $5,000).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the specific items that increase and decrease the AAA. Also, tax-exempt income is key!

reveal.question: Define the Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA) in the context of an S corporation.
reveal.answer: The Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA) is a corporate-level account that tracks the previously taxed income of an S corporation. It represents a pool of earnings that can be distributed to shareholders tax-free, up to the extent of the shareholder's basis, before distributions are considered dividends from C corporation earnings and profits (E&P), if any.

rows.label: Primary Purpose
values: Tracks previously taxed S corporation income available for tax-free distribution.
Tracks items that affect shareholder basis but not AAA, such as tax-exempt income and related expenses.

rows.label: Impact of Tax-Exempt Income
values: No impact. Tax-exempt income does NOT increase AAA.
Increased by tax-exempt income.

rows.label: Distribution Priority
values: Distributions are first considered to be from the AAA (tax-free to the extent of basis).
Distributions are NOT considered to be from the OAA until after AAA and C corporation E&P are exhausted.

title: Partnership Formation & Contributions
description: Master tax-free contributions to partnerships

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Partnership formation is generally tax-free under Section 721! Understanding when gain is recognized (services, investment partnerships) and basis calculations is fundamental for REG!

sections.title: Section 721 Nonrecognition
sections.content:
**General rule: No gain or loss on formation**

• Transfer property to partnership
• Receive partnership interest
• No immediate tax

**No 80% control requirement (unlike §351 corps)**

sections.title: Exceptions to Nonrecognition
sections.content:
**Gain recognized when:**

• Services for partnership interest
• Investment company partnership (diversification)
• Disguised sale (distribution within 2 years)
• Liability shifts trigger gain

**Services = Ordinary income (like §351)**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Partnership Formation
sections.content:
**"721 = FREE"** (mostly):

**F**ormation is tax-free
**R**eceive interest for property
**E**xceptions: Services, investment co.
**E**xcept liability issues

**721 easier than 351—no 80% control needed!**

sections.title: Partner Basis in Partnership Interest
sections.content:
**Outside basis:**

Basis of property contributed
+ Gain recognized (if any)
+ Share of partnership liabilities
= Partner's outside basis

**Liabilities increase basis (different from S corps!)**

sections.title: Partnership Basis in Contributed Property
sections.content:
**Inside basis:**

• Carryover basis from partner
• Plus any gain recognized by partner

**Holding period:**
• Tacks—includes partner's holding period
• Exception: Inventory, unrealized receivables

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Liabilities Over Basis
sections.content:
**If liability exceeds basis = GAIN!**

**Example:**
• Contribute property: Basis $50K
• Property has liability: $80K
• Gain recognized: $30K ($80K − $50K)

**Partnership assumes liability = Deemed distribution to partner**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: §721: Formation is generally tax-free
No 80% control requirement (unlike §351)
Partner basis = Property basis + liabilities assumed by partnership
Partnership basis = Carryover from partner
Liabilities assumed are deemed distribution to partner
Liabilities > Basis = Gain recognition
Services for capital interest = Ordinary income

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Alice contributes land with a basis of $50,000 and a fair market value of $80,000 to a partnership in exchange for a 25% interest. She also provides services valued at $10,000 to the partnership in exchange for an additional 5% interest. The land is subject to a mortgage of $20,000, which the partnership assumes. What is Alice's basis in her partnership interest?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Alice's basis is calculated as follows: Land basis ($50,000) + Services (Ordinary Income, $10,000) - Liability relief (25% of $20,000 mortgage) + Liability share from other partners (75% of $20,000 mortgage). The relief of $5,000 reduces her basis, but the incoming liability of $15,000 increases it. $50,000 + $10,000 - $5,000 + $5,000 = $60,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to consider both the contribution of property and services, as well as the impact of liabilities.

reveal.question: What is the general rule regarding gain or loss recognition when a partner contributes property to a partnership in exchange for a partnership interest?
reveal.answer: Generally, no gain or loss is recognized by either the partner or the partnership when property is contributed to a partnership in exchange for a partnership interest. This is under Section 721.

sections.title: Partnerships vs. S Corps: Liability Impact on Basis

rows.label: Liability Impact on Basis
values: Partner's share of partnership liabilities INCREASES their outside basis.
Shareholder's share of S corporation liabilities generally DOES NOT increase their stock basis, unless the shareholder directly guarantees the debt and is economically at risk.

rows.label: Direct Impact on Basis
values: Direct impact on outside basis
Indirect impact (through at-risk rules)

title: Partnership Partner Basis Calculations
description: Master inside and outside basis for partners

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Partner basis determines deductible losses and distribution treatment! Unlike S corps, partnership debt increases basis. Understanding inside vs. outside basis is critical for REG!

sections.title: Outside vs. Inside Basis
sections.content:
**Two types of basis:**

**Outside basis:**
• Partner's basis in partnership interest
• Used for loss limitations and distribution treatment

**Inside basis:**
• Partnership's basis in its assets
• Used for depreciation and gain/loss on sale

sections.title: Initial Outside Basis
sections.content:
**Partner's starting basis:**

Cash contributed
+ FMV of services (if recognized)
+ Adjusted basis of property contributed
+ Share of partnership liabilities
= Initial outside basis

**Liabilities are key difference from S corps!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Outside Basis
sections.content:
**"CLIP"** for outside basis:

**C**ontributed property basis
**L**iabilities (share of)
**I**ncome increases
**P**artnership distributions decrease

**CLIP your outside basis!**

sections.title: Liability Allocation
sections.content:
**Recourse liabilities:**
• Allocated to partners bearing economic risk of loss

**Nonrecourse liabilities:**
• Generally allocated by profit-sharing ratios
• Minimum gain allocations may apply

**All liabilities increase basis (big advantage over S corps!)**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Partnership vs. S Corp Debt
sections.content:
**Partnership debt = Basis; S corp debt = NO basis**

**Partnership:**
• Bank loan → Increases partner basis
• Can deduct losses against share of debt

**S Corporation:**
• Bank loan → NO shareholder basis
• Only direct shareholder loans count

**Huge difference for loss deductions!**

sections.title: Basis Cannot Go Below Zero
sections.content:
**Ordering rule prevents negative basis:**

1. Increase for income
2. Decrease for distributions
3. Decrease for non-deductible items
4. Decrease for losses

**Excess losses suspended and carried forward**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Outside basis = Partner's basis in partnership interest
Inside basis = Partnership's basis in assets
ALL partnership debt increases partner basis
Recourse debt: To partners with economic risk
Nonrecourse debt: Generally by profit ratios
Major advantage over S corps for loss deductions
Losses limited to outside basis; excess carries forward

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Outside vs. Inside Basis: Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Partner A contributes land with an adjusted basis of $50,000 and a fair market value of $80,000 to a partnership. He also assumes a $20,000 share of partnership liabilities. For purposes of determining Partner A's initial outside basis in the partnership, what amount is used with respect to the land?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The adjusted basis of the property contributed ($50,000) is used to determine the partner's initial outside basis. The fair market value is relevant for determining capital account balances, but not the tax basis.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the difference between basis and fair market value when calculating a partner's outside basis upon contribution.

reveal.question: What is the formula to calculate a partner's initial outside basis in a partnership?
reveal.answer: Cash contributed + FMV of services (if recognized) + Adjusted basis of property contributed + Share of partnership liabilities = Initial outside basis

rows.label: Change
values: Increased by partner's share of partnership income and increased debt; decreased by distributions and partner's share of partnership losses and decreased debt.
Fluctuates with partnership asset transactions, depreciation, etc.

title: Partnership Allocations & Distributions
description: Understand special allocations and distribution rules

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Partnerships offer flexibility to allocate income and deductions specially! But allocations must have substantial economic effect. Understanding current vs. liquidating distributions is essential for REG!

sections.title: Partnership Allocations
sections.content:
**How income/loss is divided:**

• Generally follows partnership agreement
• Must have "substantial economic effect" (SEE)
• Otherwise, allocated by partners' interests

**Flexibility is partnership's key advantage!**

sections.title: Substantial Economic Effect
sections.content:
**Two requirements for SEE:**

1. **Economic effect:**
   • Capital accounts maintained properly
   • Liquidating distributions by capital accounts
   • Deficit restoration or QIO

2. **Substantiality:**
   • Reasonable possibility allocation affects dollar amounts
   • Not just tax shifting

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Distributions
sections.content:
**"CLO"** for current distributions:

**C**ash first reduces basis
**L**oss only if cash > basis
**O**ther property = No gain (usually)

**Liquidating distributions are MORE complex!**

sections.title: Liquidating Distributions
sections.content:
**When partner exits:**

**Cash only:**
• Gain if cash > basis
• Loss if cash < basis

**Property:**
• Basis = Partner's remaining outside basis
• Allocated among assets received

**Loss only recognized in cash/unrealized receivables/inventory liquidations**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Hot Assets (§751)
sections.content:
**Hot assets = Ordinary income!**

**Hot assets include:**
• Unrealized receivables
• Inventory items
• Depreciation recapture

**Distribution of hot assets:**
• May trigger ordinary income
• Even if overall partnership has capital gain character

**§751 recharacterizes gain as ordinary!**

sections.title: Disguised Sales
sections.content:
**Contribution + distribution = Sale?**

**Presumed sale if within 2 years:**
• Partner contributes property
• Partner receives cash/property distribution
• Treated as sale (not tax-free contribution)

**Can rebut with facts showing legitimate business purpose**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Allocations must have substantial economic effect
Current distributions: Generally no gain unless cash > basis
Property distributions: Carryover basis (limited)
Liquidating distributions: Gain/loss may be recognized
Hot assets (§751): Ordinary income treatment
Disguised sale rules: Watch 2-year period
Loss on liquidation: Only for cash/inventory/receivables

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a requirement for a partnership allocation to have substantial economic effect (SEE)?
options: Capital accounts are maintained according to partnership regulations.
Liquidating distributions are made in accordance with positive capital account balances.
The allocation has a valid business purpose independent of tax consequences.
Partners have either a deficit restoration obligation (DRO) or a Qualified Income Offset (QIO).
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While a valid business purpose is generally a good idea, it is not specifically a requirement for an allocation to have substantial economic effect. The three required components are proper capital account maintenance, liquidation distributions based on capital accounts, and either a deficit restoration obligation or a qualified income offset.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the two main components of SEE: economic effect and substantiality. Which answer doesn't fit into either of those categories?

reveal.question: What is a Deficit Restoration Obligation (DRO) in the context of partnership allocations?
reveal.answer: A Deficit Restoration Obligation (DRO) is a requirement in a partnership agreement that obligates a partner to contribute cash to the partnership to restore a deficit balance in their capital account upon liquidation. This ensures that the partner ultimately bears the economic burden of their share of partnership losses.

rows.label: Purpose
values: To restore a deficit capital account balance upon liquidation.
To prevent a deficit capital account balance from occurring due to unexpected allocations.

rows.label: Mechanism
values: Partner is obligated to contribute cash to the partnership.
Requires that the partner be allocated items of income and gain in an amount sufficient to eliminate the unexpected deficit as quickly as possible.

rows.label: Timing
values: Applies at liquidation.
Applies during the tax year when the unexpected allocation occurs, or as soon as possible thereafter.

title: Partnership Sales & Liquidations
description: Master the tax consequences of selling partnership interests

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Selling a partnership interest involves complex calculations! Hot assets trigger ordinary income, and Section 754 elections affect both buyer and seller. Understanding these rules is critical for REG!

sections.title: Sale of Partnership Interest
sections.content:
**General rule: Capital gain/loss**

**Calculation:**
Amount realized
− Adjusted outside basis
= Gain or loss

**Exception: Hot assets → Ordinary income**

sections.title: Amount Realized
sections.content:
**Amount realized includes:**

• Cash received
• FMV of property received
• Buyer's assumption of selling partner's share of liabilities

**Liabilities increase amount realized!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Sale Components
sections.content:
**"CAROL"** for sale calculation:

**C**ash received
**A**ssumed liabilities (by buyer)
**R**educed by basis
**O**rdinary income for hot assets
**L**ong-term capital gain (remainder)

**Don't forget liabilities in amount realized!**

sections.title: Section 754 Election
sections.content:
**Optional adjustment to inside basis:**

**Purpose:**
• Adjust partnership's inside basis
• To reflect buyer's purchase price

**Calculations:**
• §743(b): Transfer of interest
• §734(b): Distribution of property

**Election applies to all future transfers!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Mandatory Basis Adjustment
sections.content:
**§754 election is MANDATORY if:**

• Substantial basis reduction (>$250,000)
• Substantial built-in loss

**Previously optional, now required in these situations!**

**Prevents abuse by shifting losses to new partners**

sections.title: Liquidation of Partnership
sections.content:
**Complete liquidation:**

• Partners receive liquidating distributions
• Gain/loss recognized on cash vs. basis
• Property takes remaining basis

**Partial liquidation:**
• Treated as distribution + redemption
• Hot asset rules apply

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sale of interest: Generally capital gain/loss
Amount realized includes assumed liabilities
Hot assets (§751): Ordinary income treatment
§754 election adjusts inside basis
Mandatory adjustment for substantial basis reductions
Liquidation: Cash vs. basis determines recognition
Partnership interest sale is entity approach (not aggregate)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Partner Able sells his partnership interest to Baker for $75,000 cash and the assumption of Able's share of partnership liabilities totaling $25,000. Able's adjusted basis in his partnership interest is $60,000.  Assuming no hot assets are involved, what is Able's gain or loss on the sale?
options: $15,000 Capital Gain
$40,000 Capital Gain
$15,000 Ordinary Income
$40,000 Ordinary Income
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The amount realized is $75,000 (cash) + $25,000 (liability relief) = $100,000. The gain is $100,000 (amount realized) - $60,000 (adjusted basis) = $40,000.  Since no hot assets were involved, this is a capital gain.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to include the liability relief in the amount realized.

reveal.question: List the three components that make up the amount realized when selling a partnership interest.
reveal.answer: The amount realized includes: 1) Cash received, 2) Fair Market Value of property received, and 3) Buyer's assumption of selling partner's share of liabilities.

knowledgeCheck.question: When a partnership interest is sold, the presence of 'hot assets' can trigger:
options: A decrease in the partner's capital account.
Ordinary income recognition for a portion of the gain.
A revaluation of the partnership's assets.
An adjustment to the partnership's inside basis.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The presence of 'hot assets' (such as unrealized receivables and substantially appreciated inventory) requires the selling partner to recognize ordinary income to the extent the sale relates to those assets. This is an exception to the general rule of capital gain/loss treatment.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what kind of income 'hot assets' typically generate when realized by the partnership.

title: Trusts & Estates: Fiduciary Income Tax
description: Understand taxation of trusts and estates

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Trusts and estates are separate taxpayers with unique rules! Income can be taxed to the entity or beneficiaries. Understanding DNI and the distribution deduction is essential for REG!

sections.title: Fiduciary Taxation Overview
sections.content:
**Trusts and estates file Form 1041:**

• Separate taxable entities
• Income taxed to entity OR beneficiaries
• Distribution deduction passes income through
• Highly compressed tax brackets

**$14,450 income = 37% bracket (2024)!**

sections.title: Types of Trusts
rows: May accumulate income or distribute corpus
Grantor taxed on income (ignored for tax)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Simple vs. Complex
sections.content:
**"SIMPLE = SIMPLE"**

**S**imple trusts
**I**ncome distributed currently
**M**ust distribute (no accumulation)
**P**rincipal stays in trust
**L**imited to income distributions
**E**xemption: $300

**COMPLEX = Everything else (and $100 exemption)**

sections.title: Fiduciary Taxable Income
sections.content:
**Similar to individual calculation:**

Gross income
− Deductions (administration, etc.)
− Distribution deduction
− Personal exemption ($300 or $100)
= Taxable income

**Key: Distribution deduction flows income to beneficiaries**

sections.title: Distributable Net Income (DNI)
sections.content:
**DNI limits distribution deduction:**

• Maximum deduction = DNI
• Maximum taxable to beneficiaries = DNI
• Preserves character of income

**DNI = Modified taxable income of trust/estate**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Compressed Brackets
sections.content:
**Trust/estate brackets hit 37% FAST!**

**2024:**
• 10%: $0 - $3,100
• 24%: $3,100 - $11,150
• 35%: $11,150 - $14,450
• 37%: Over $14,450

**Compare to individuals: 37% at ~$609K!**

**Strong incentive to distribute income to beneficiaries**

sections.title: Income in Respect of Decedent (IRD)
sections.content:
**Income earned by decedent but not taxed:**

• Examples: Unpaid salary, deferred comp, IRA distributions
• No step-up in basis (exception to general rule)
• Taxed to estate or beneficiary who receives it
• May get deduction for estate tax paid on IRD

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Trusts/estates are separate taxpayers (Form 1041)
Distribution deduction passes income to beneficiaries
DNI limits both deduction and beneficiary inclusion
Compressed brackets: 37% at $14,450
Simple trusts: Must distribute income; $300 exemption
Complex trusts: May accumulate; $100 exemption
IRD: No step-up; taxed when received

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A trust has gross income of $50,000, deductible administration expenses of $5,000, and a distribution deduction of $20,000. What is the trust's taxable income before the personal exemption?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Taxable income is calculated as gross income less deductions (administration expenses and distribution deduction). $50,000 (Gross Income) - $5,000 (Administration) - $20,000 (Distribution Deduction) = $25,000. The question asks for income BEFORE the personal exemption.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: Gross Income - Deductions - Distribution Deduction - Personal Exemption = Taxable Income

reveal.question: What is a key characteristic of fiduciary income tax brackets?
reveal.answer: Fiduciary income tax brackets are highly compressed, meaning even a relatively small amount of income can push the trust or estate into a high tax bracket (e.g., 37% for $14,450 in 2024). This necessitates careful planning regarding distributions.

rows.label: Personal Exemption
values: $300 (simple trust) or $100 (complex trust/estate)
Varies based on filing status and dependency

rows.label: Distribution Deduction
values: Yes, for amounts distributed to beneficiaries
No

rows.label: Tax Brackets
values: Highly compressed; high rates at low income levels
Wider brackets; progressive rates

title: Distributable Net Income (DNI)
description: Master DNI calculations and character

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: DNI is the ceiling for both the distribution deduction and beneficiary income! Understanding how to calculate DNI and how it preserves character is essential for fiduciary taxation on REG!

sections.title: Purpose of DNI
sections.content:
**DNI serves multiple purposes:**

1. **Ceiling on distribution deduction** (for trust/estate)
2. **Ceiling on beneficiary inclusion**
3. **Preserves character** of income

**DNI = Tax on either entity OR beneficiaries, not both**

sections.title: DNI Calculation
sections.content:
**Start with taxable income, then adjust:**

Taxable income
+ Personal exemption (add back)
+ Net tax-exempt interest (less expenses)
− Capital gains allocated to corpus
+ Capital losses (if deducted)
= Distributable Net Income

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: DNI Adjustments
sections.content:
**"PECK"** for DNI:

**P**ersonal exemption added back
**E**xempt interest included (net)
**C**apital gains removed (if to corpus)
**K**eep character of income

**DNI = Economic income available to distribute**

sections.title: Tier System
sections.content:
**How DNI is allocated among beneficiaries:**

**Tier 1:** Required distributions (income)
• Gets first share of DNI

**Tier 2:** Discretionary distributions
• Shares remaining DNI pro rata

**Tier 1 fills first, then Tier 2**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Capital Gains in DNI
sections.content:
**Capital gains are usually NOT in DNI!**

**General rule:**
• Capital gains allocated to corpus
• Not included in DNI
• Taxed to trust/estate

**Exception:**
• If trust document or local law allocates to income
• If actually distributed to beneficiary

**Default: Capital gains stay with trust!**

sections.title: Separate Share Rule
sections.content:
**Complex trusts with separate shares:**

• Treat each share as separate trust
• DNI calculated separately
• Distributions only carry out that share's DNI

**Prevents shifting income between beneficiaries**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: DNI = Ceiling for distribution deduction and beneficiary income
Start with taxable income, make specific adjustments
Tax-exempt income included in DNI (net)
Capital gains usually excluded (allocated to corpus)
Character of income preserved through DNI
Tier 1 (required) filled before Tier 2 (discretionary)
Separate share rule may apply

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a primary purpose of Distributable Net Income (DNI)?
options: To act as a ceiling on the distribution deduction for a trust or estate.
To act as a ceiling on the amount of income a beneficiary must include in their gross income.
To determine the amount of income tax paid by the trust or estate.
To preserve the character of the income (e.g., tax-exempt interest) distributed to beneficiaries.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: DNI does not directly determine the amount of income tax paid by the trust or estate. It helps determine how much income is taxed at the beneficiary level versus the trust/estate level. The tax itself is calculated on taxable income after considering the distribution deduction (which is limited by DNI).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what DNI *limits*, not what it *calculates*.

reveal.question: What is the FIRST step in calculating Distributable Net Income (DNI)?
reveal.answer: Start with the taxable income of the trust or estate.

knowledgeCheck.question: A trust's taxable income is $50,000. It has a $300 personal exemption, $2,000 of net tax-exempt interest, and $5,000 of capital gains allocated to corpus. What is the trust's DNI?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: DNI = Taxable income + Personal exemption + Net tax-exempt interest - Capital gains allocated to corpus = $50,000 + $300 + $2,000 - $5,000 = $47,300
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the signs! Add back the personal exemption and tax-exempt interest, and subtract capital gains allocated to corpus.

title: Simple vs. Complex Trusts
description: Understand the differences and tax treatment

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Trust classification affects taxation! Simple trusts must distribute all income and get different treatment than complex trusts. Understanding these distinctions is important for REG!

sections.title: Simple Trust Requirements
sections.content:
**ALL three must be met:**

1. Trust instrument requires ALL income distributed currently
2. No amounts distributed from corpus
3. No charitable contributions made by trust

**Fail ANY requirement = Complex trust**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Simple Trust
sections.content:
**"ALL-NONE-NONE"** for simple:

**ALL** income must be distributed
**NONE** of corpus can be distributed
**NONE** to charity

**If it's not ALL-NONE-NONE, it's COMPLEX!**

sections.title: Simple Trust Taxation
sections.content:
**Beneficiary always taxed:**

• Trust gets deduction for DNI distributed
• Beneficiary includes DNI in income
• Character passes through
• Trust pays tax only on capital gains (to corpus)

**$300 exemption for simple trusts**

sections.title: Complex Trust Taxation
sections.content:
**More flexibility, more complexity:**

• Distributions may or may not be made
• Accumulated income taxed to trust
• Distributed income taxed to beneficiaries
• Can make charitable contributions (deductible)

**$100 exemption for complex trusts**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Year-to-Year Classification
sections.content:
**Same trust can change classification!**

**Example:**
• Year 1: All income distributed, no corpus = Simple
• Year 2: Corpus distributed = Complex (that year)

**Test requirements EACH year separately!**

**Classification is annual, not permanent**

sections.title: 65-Day Rule
sections.content:
**Complex trusts only:**

• Distributions in first 65 days of year
• Can elect to treat as prior year distribution
• Helps with tax planning

**Not available for simple trusts (must distribute anyway)**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Simple trust: All income distributed, no corpus, no charity
Complex trust: Everything else
Simple exemption: $300; Complex exemption: $100
Classification can change year to year
Simple trusts: Beneficiaries taxed on DNI
Complex trusts: Trust OR beneficiaries taxed
65-day rule: Complex trusts can elect prior year treatment

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following characteristics would disqualify a trust from being classified as a simple trust for federal income tax purposes?
options: The trust instrument requires all income to be distributed currently.
The trust makes distributions from corpus during the year.
The trust does not make any charitable contributions.
The trust earns both ordinary income and capital gains.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A simple trust cannot distribute corpus (principal). The other characteristics are consistent with the definition of a simple trust. A simple trust MUST distribute all income currently, cannot make charitable contributions, and the type of income it earns (ordinary vs. capital gains) is not relevant to its classification.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the core requirements of a simple trust regarding distributions.

reveal.question: What is the exemption amount for a simple trust?
reveal.answer: $300. Simple trusts are allowed a $300 exemption when calculating taxable income.

rows.label: Income Distribution
values: Required to distribute all income currently
May accumulate income

rows.label: Corpus Distribution
values: Cannot distribute corpus
May distribute corpus

rows.label: Charitable Contributions
values: Cannot make charitable contributions
May make charitable contributions

knowledgeCheck.question: A simple trust has $50,000 of DNI and distributes $50,000 to its sole beneficiary. The trust also realizes a $10,000 capital gain. How is the capital gain taxed?
options: The beneficiary is taxed on the $10,000 capital gain.
The trust is taxed on the $10,000 capital gain.
The capital gain is split proportionally between the beneficiary and the trust.
The capital gain is not taxed.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Capital gains are generally taxed to the trust (to corpus). Even though the income is fully distributed, capital gains are an exception in a simple trust.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember who pays the tax on capital gains in a simple trust.

title: Tax-Exempt Entities: Section 501(c)(3)
description: Understand requirements for tax-exempt organizations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: 501(c)(3) organizations are exempt from income tax AND can receive deductible contributions! Understanding the requirements and restrictions is important for REG and for advising nonprofit clients!

sections.title: 501(c)(3) Requirements
sections.content:
**Must be organized and operated exclusively for:**

• Religious
• Charitable
• Scientific
• Educational
• Literary
• Prevention of cruelty to children/animals
• Fostering amateur sports competition

sections.title: Organizational Test
sections.content:
**Organizing documents must:**

• Limit purposes to exempt purposes
• Not permit private benefit
• Provide for distribution to exempt org on dissolution

**Look to articles of incorporation/trust document**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: 501(c)(3) Types
sections.content:
**"RECELS"** for exempt purposes:

**R**eligious
**E**ducational
**C**haritable
**E**radicate cruelty
**L**iterary
**S**cientific

**RECELS get tax exemption!**

sections.title: Operational Test
sections.content:
**Must actually operate for exempt purposes:**

• Primary activities further exempt purpose
• No private inurement (benefits to insiders)
• No substantial lobbying
• No political campaign activity

**What you DO matters, not just what documents say**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Political Activity
sections.content:
**501(c)(3) = NO political campaign activity!**

**Prohibited:**
• Endorsing candidates
• Contributing to campaigns
• Making political statements

**Limited lobbying allowed:**
• Insubstantial part of activities
• Or elect 501(h) expenditure test

**Political activity = REVOCATION!**

sections.title: Public Charity vs. Private Foundation
sections.content:
**501(c)(3)s are either:**

**Public charity:**
• Broad public support
• Less restrictive rules
• Preferred status

**Private foundation:**
• Limited funding sources
• Excise taxes apply
• More restrictions on activities

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 501(c)(3): Exempt from tax + deductible contributions
Organizational test: Documents limit to exempt purposes
Operational test: Actually operate for exempt purposes
No private inurement to insiders
No political campaign activity (absolute)
Limited lobbying allowed
Public charity preferred over private foundation

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following provisions, if included in a 501(c)(3) organization's articles of incorporation, would disqualify it from tax-exempt status?
options: A statement limiting the organization's purposes to charitable and educational activities.
A provision allowing the organization to distribute its assets to its members upon dissolution.
A clause prohibiting private inurement to board members.
A requirement that the organization's activities be primarily educational.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A 501(c)(3) organization's articles of incorporation must provide for the distribution of assets to another exempt organization upon dissolution. Distributing assets to members constitutes private benefit, which is prohibited.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what happens to the assets of a charity if it shuts down.

reveal.question: List at least four of the exempt purposes that qualify an organization for 501(c)(3) status.
reveal.answer: The exempt purposes include: Religious, Charitable, Scientific, Educational, Literary, Prevention of cruelty to children/animals, and Fostering amateur sports competition.

rows.label: Focus
values: Governing Documents
Actual Activities

rows.label: Key Requirements
values: Limited to exempt purposes, No private benefit, Distribution to exempt org on dissolution
Primary activities further exempt purpose, No private inurement, No substantial lobbying, No political campaign activity

rows.label: Evidence
values: Articles of Incorporation/Trust Document
Day-to-day operations, Programs, Financial records

title: Unrelated Business Income (UBTI)
description: Understand when exempt organizations pay tax

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Even tax-exempt organizations pay tax on unrelated business income! Understanding what triggers UBTI prevents surprises and helps exempt organizations plan activities. This is a popular REG topic!

sections.title: UBTI Definition
sections.content:
**Three requirements for UBTI:**

1. **Trade or business** (profit motive)
2. **Regularly carried on**
3. **Not substantially related** to exempt purpose

**All three must be met = UBTI**

sections.title: Trade or Business Test
sections.content:
**Activity conducted for profit:**

• Same standards as for-profit entities
• Intent to profit (even if loses money)
• Selling goods or services

**Investment activities generally NOT trade or business**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: UBTI Tests
sections.content:
**"RUN"** to avoid UBTI:

**R**elated to exempt purpose
**U**nregularly carried on
**N**ot a trade or business

**Meet ANY of these = NOT UBTI**

**Fail all three = RUN to Form 990-T!**

sections.title: Substantially Related
sections.content:
**Activity must CONTRIBUTE to exempt purpose:**

• More than just generating revenue
• Conduct itself furthers mission
• Size and extent must be proportionate

**Just funding exempt activities is NOT enough!**

sections.title: Excluded from UBTI
sections.content:
**Statutory exclusions:**

• Volunteer labor (substantially all)
• Donated merchandise sales
• Member convenience activities
• Dividends, interest, royalties (generally)
• Rental income (generally)
• Research income

**Know these common exclusions!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Debt-Financed Property
sections.content:
**Rental income from debt-financed property = UBTI!**

**Example:**
• Church owns rental building
• Building has mortgage
• Rental income proportionally taxable

**Formula:**
(Average acquisition indebtedness / Average adjusted basis) × Rental income = UBTI

**Even passive income taxable if debt-financed!**

sections.title: UBTI Tax Calculation
sections.content:
**Form 990-T:**

• $1,000 specific deduction
• Taxed at corporate rates (21%)
• Losses can offset (with limitations)
• Separate "silos" for certain losses

**Estimated taxes may be required**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: UBTI: Trade/business + Regularly carried on + Unrelated
All three tests must be met for UBTI
Exclusions: Volunteers, donations, passive income
Debt-financed property income may be UBTI
Form 990-T: $1,000 deduction, taxed at 21%
Generating revenue alone doesn't make activity related
Common activities: Gift shops, parking, advertising

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: An exempt organization operates a gift shop that sells items related to its exempt purpose (e.g., a museum selling books about art). It also runs a parking lot primarily for visitors, but also allows employees to use it for a fee. Which of the following activities, if any, is MOST likely to generate Unrelated Business Income (UBI)?
options: The sale of books about art in the gift shop.
The operation of the parking lot.
Both the sale of books and the operation of the parking lot.
Neither the sale of books nor the operation of the parking lot.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The parking lot is regularly carried on and not substantially related to the exempt purpose (assuming the exempt purpose isn't parking-related). The sale of art-related books is substantially related to the museum's exempt purpose. Therefore, only the parking lot is likely to generate UBI.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which activity is MOST directly related to the organization's exempt purpose.

reveal.question: List the three requirements that MUST be met for an activity to be considered Unrelated Business Income (UBTI).
reveal.answer:
1. It is a trade or business.
2. It is regularly carried on.
3. It is not substantially related to the organization's exempt purpose.

sections.title: Trade or Business vs. Investment Activities

rows.label: Profit Motive
values: Primary purpose is to generate profit.
Profit is a goal, but activities may be passive (e.g., dividends, interest).

rows.label: Level of Activity
values: Active involvement in operations.
Generally passive, requiring minimal management effort.

rows.label: UBTI Implications
values: Can generate UBTI if the other two requirements are met.
Generally do not generate UBTI.

rows.label: Examples
values: Operating a retail store or providing services for a fee.
Collecting dividends from stock investments.

title: Partnership Liquidations and Terminations
description: Master partnership wind-up tax rules

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Partnerships eventually end! Understanding liquidating distributions vs. sales, and when a partnership terminates for tax purposes, is essential. These rules differ significantly from current distributions!

sections.title: Partnership Termination
sections.content:
**A partnership terminates when:**

• No part of business continues to be carried on
• By any partner
• In a partnership

**TCJA eliminated technical terminations!**

**Sale of 50%+ interest no longer terminates partnership**

sections.title: Liquidating Distribution Rules
sections.content:
**Gain recognized when:**
• Cash exceeds outside basis

**Loss recognized when:**
• Only money, inventory, unrealized receivables received
• FMV less than outside basis

**Property distributions:**
• Basis = Carryover (limited to outside basis)
• Generally no gain/loss

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Liquidation Loss
sections.content:
**"MIR" = Loss possible**

**M**oney only
**I**nventory only
**R**eceivables only

**Get MIR items + FMV < basis = Recognize LOSS**

**Get ANY other property = NO loss recognition**

sections.title: Section 736 Payments
sections.content:
**Payments to retiring/deceased partner:**

**736(a): Income payments**
• Distributive share or guaranteed payments
• Ordinary income to recipient
• Deductible to partnership

**736(b): Property payments**
• For partner's interest in partnership assets
• Exchange treatment to partner
• No deduction to partnership

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Hot Assets
sections.content:
**Hot assets = Ordinary income!**

**Hot assets include:**
• Unrealized receivables
• Substantially appreciated inventory

**Section 751(b):** Ordinary gain if:
• Hot assets exchanged for non-hot
• Or vice versa

**Cannot convert ordinary income to capital gain!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Partnership terminates when business ceases entirely
TCJA: 50%+ sale no longer terminates
Liquidating loss: Only if receive MIR (money, inventory, receivables)
736(a): Income items; 736(b): Property items
Hot assets trigger ordinary income treatment
Cannot convert ordinary income to capital gain via distribution

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following events, standing alone, will NOT result in the termination of a partnership for federal income tax purposes?
options: No part of any business continues to be carried on by any of its partners in a partnership.
The sale of 55% of the total interest in partnership capital and profits within a 12-month period.
The partnership's activities are discontinued.
All partners decide to discontinue the partnership
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The sale of 50% or more of the total interest in partnership capital and profits within a 12-month period used to trigger a technical termination. However, the TCJA eliminated technical terminations. The other options describe scenarios where the partnership ceases to exist.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the impact of the TCJA on partnership termination rules.

reveal.question: Under what circumstances does a partner recognize a gain on a liquidating distribution?
reveal.answer: A partner recognizes a gain on a liquidating distribution only to the extent that the cash received exceeds the partner's outside basis in the partnership interest. Property distributions generally do not trigger gain recognition.

knowledgeCheck.question: A partner's outside basis in a partnership is $50,000. In a complete liquidation of the partner's interest, the partner receives $10,000 cash, unrealized receivables with a basis of $0 and fair market value of $5,000, and inventory with a basis of $10,000 and fair market value of $12,000. What is the amount of the partner's recognized loss?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A partner recognizes a loss when only money, unrealized receivables, and inventory are received in liquidation, and the total basis of these assets is less than the partner's outside basis. In this case, the partner received $10,000 cash, $0 basis unrealized receivables, and $10,000 basis inventory, totaling $20,000. The loss is $50,000 (outside basis) - $10,000 (cash) - $0 (receivables) - $10,000 (inventory) = $30,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the basis of the assets received, not their fair market value. Only money, inventory, and unrealized receivables are relevant for loss recognition.

title: Gift Tax Fundamentals
description: Master federal gift tax rules and exclusions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Gift tax is part of the unified transfer tax system! Understanding annual exclusions, lifetime exemptions, and what constitutes a taxable gift is essential for estate planning and REG. Many candidates overlook gift tax!

sections.title: Gift Tax Overview
sections.content:
**Unified transfer tax system:**

• Gift tax (lifetime transfers)
• Estate tax (death transfers)
• Generation-skipping tax (to skip persons)

**Gift tax is paid by DONOR, not donee!**

**Unified credit shelters both gift and estate taxes**

sections.title: What is a Gift?
sections.content:
**Transfer without adequate consideration:**

• Voluntary
• Without full value in return
• Complete transfer (donor gives up control)

**Incomplete gift = No current gift tax**

**Below-market loans may be deemed gifts (imputed interest)**
